title: Financial Statements Overview
description: Master the purpose, components, and interrelationships of the primary financial statements

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: As a CMA, you'll prepare, analyze, and use financial statements for strategic decision-making. Understanding how statements connect and what story they tell is FUNDAMENTAL to every aspect of your role - from budgeting to performance evaluation to investor relations.

sections.title: The Four Primary Financial Statements
rows: What do we own and owe?
Where did cash come from and go?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Financial Statement Cycle
sections.content:
**"BICE"** - How statements flow together:

**B**alance Sheet (beginning) → 
**I**ncome Statement (period activity) → 
**C**ash Flow Statement (cash reconciliation) → 
**E**quity Statement (ownership changes) → 
**B**alance Sheet (ending)

Net income flows from Income Statement to Retained Earnings. Cash flows to the Balance Sheet cash account.

sections.title: Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position)
sections.content:
**The Fundamental Equation:**
Assets = Liabilities + Stockholders' Equity

**Asset Classification:**
• **Current Assets:** Cash, receivables, inventory, prepaid expenses (converted to cash within 1 year)
• **Non-current Assets:** PP&E, intangibles, long-term investments

**Liability Classification:**
• **Current Liabilities:** Payables, accrued expenses, current portion of LT debt (due within 1 year)
• **Non-current Liabilities:** Long-term debt, deferred taxes, pension obligations

**Stockholders' Equity:**
• Contributed capital (common stock, APIC)
• Retained earnings
• Accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI)
• Treasury stock (contra-equity)

sections.title: Income Statement
sections.content:
**Single-Step Format:**
Total Revenues - Total Expenses = Net Income

**Multi-Step Format (More Common):**
```
Net Sales
- Cost of Goods Sold
= Gross Profit
- Operating Expenses
= Operating Income
± Other Income/Expenses
= Income Before Tax
- Income Tax Expense
= Net Income from Continuing Operations
± Discontinued Operations (net of tax)
= Net Income
```

**Key Subtotals:**
• Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit / Net Sales
• Operating Margin = Operating Income / Net Sales
• Net Profit Margin = Net Income / Net Sales

sections.title: Statement of Cash Flows
sections.content:
**Three Sections:**

**Operating Activities:**
• Cash from core business operations
• Start with net income, adjust for non-cash items
• Changes in working capital accounts

**Investing Activities:**
• Capital expenditures (PP&E purchases)
• Investments in securities
• Proceeds from asset sales

**Financing Activities:**
• Borrowing and repaying debt
• Issuing and repurchasing stock
• Dividend payments

**The Reconciliation:**
Beginning Cash + Operating + Investing + Financing = Ending Cash

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Direct vs. Indirect Method
sections.content:
**The CMA heavily tests the INDIRECT METHOD for operating cash flows!**

**Start with Net Income, then adjust:**
• ADD back non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization)
• ADD/SUBTRACT gains/losses on asset sales
• ADJUST for changes in current assets and liabilities

**Rule of thumb:**
• Current asset INCREASE = cash DECREASE (subtract)
• Current liability INCREASE = cash INCREASE (add)

**Example:** AR increased $10,000 → Subtract $10,000 (sold on credit, no cash yet)

sections.title: Statement of Stockholders' Equity
sections.content:
**Components Reconciled:**
• Common Stock
• Additional Paid-in Capital
• Retained Earnings
• Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income
• Treasury Stock

**Common Transactions:**
• Net income → Increases retained earnings
• Dividends declared → Decreases retained earnings
• Stock issuance → Increases common stock and APIC
• Stock repurchase → Increases treasury stock (reduces equity)
• OCI items → Change AOCI directly (bypass income statement)

sections.title: Articulation: How Statements Connect
content: Net Income from Income Statement → Retained Earnings (SE Statement) → Balance Sheet
Ending Cash from Cash Flow Statement → Cash on Balance Sheet
Dividends declared → Reduces Retained Earnings
PP&E purchases (Investing CF) → Increases PP&E on Balance Sheet
Debt issuance (Financing CF) → Increases Liabilities on Balance Sheet
items: **Net Income acts as the bridge**: Bottom of Income Statement flows to Statement of Retained Earnings.
**Retained Earnings link**: Ending Retained Earnings flows to Equity section of Balance Sheet.
**Cash link**: Ending Cash on Statement of Cash Flows matches Cash on Balance Sheet.
**Non-Cash items**: Depreciation on IS adds back to Operating Cash Flows; accumulated depreciation reduces Asset book value on BS.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four statements: Balance Sheet, Income Statement, Cash Flows, Changes in Equity
Balance Sheet shows position at a point in time; others show changes over a period
Assets = Liabilities + Equity (always balances)
Cash flow statement uses indirect method most often (start with net income)
Increase in current assets = cash outflow; increase in current liabilities = cash inflow
Statements articulate - net income flows to retained earnings to balance sheet
Multi-step income statement shows gross profit and operating income subtotals

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following accounts would be classified as a current asset on the balance sheet?
options: Land
Accounts Receivable
Buildings
Goodwill
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Accounts Receivable represents amounts owed to the company by customers, which are expected to be collected within one year, making it a current asset. Land, Buildings, and Goodwill are all non-current assets.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the definition of a current asset: converted to cash within one year.

reveal.question: What is the primary difference between the single-step and multi-step income statement formats?
reveal.answer: The single-step format calculates net income by subtracting total expenses from total revenues in one step. The multi-step format calculates several intermediate subtotals such as Gross Profit and Operating Income before arriving at Net Income. The multi-step format is generally considered more informative.

rows.label: Time Horizon
values: Expected to be converted to cash or used up within one year or the operating cycle, whichever is longer.
Expected to benefit the company for more than one year.

rows.label: Examples
values: Cash, Accounts Receivable, Inventory, Prepaid Expenses
Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E), Intangible Assets, Long-term Investments

rows.label: Liquidity
values: More liquid
Less liquid

title: Revenue Recognition (ASC 606)
description: Apply the five-step model for recognizing revenue from contracts with customers

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Revenue is THE most important line on the income statement! As a CMA, you'll help structure deals, determine when to recognize revenue, and ensure compliance. Misapplying ASC 606 leads to restatements, SEC enforcement, and career-ending mistakes.

sections.title: The Core Principle
sections.content:
**ASC 606 Core Principle:**
Recognize revenue to depict the transfer of promised goods or services to customers in an amount that reflects the consideration the entity expects to be entitled to in exchange for those goods or services.

**Translation:** Recognize revenue when you've EARNED it by delivering what you promised, for the amount you expect to collect.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Five Steps
sections.content:
**"I PROMISE TO DELIVER VALUE"**

**Step 1:** **I**dentify the contract
**Step 2:** **P**erformance obligations identified
**Step 3:** **T**ransaction price determined
**Step 4:** **A**llocate price to obligations
**Step 5:** **R**ecognize revenue when satisfied

Or simply: **IPTAR** - Identify, PO, Transaction, Allocate, Recognize

sections.title: Step 1: Identify the Contract
sections.content:
**A contract exists when ALL criteria are met:**

1. **Approval:** Parties have approved the contract
2. **Rights:** Each party's rights are identified
3. **Payment terms:** Payment terms are identified
4. **Commercial substance:** The contract has commercial substance
5. **Collectibility:** Collection of consideration is probable

**No contract?** No revenue recognition until criteria are met. Consider whether it's a deposit/liability.

sections.title: Step 2: Identify Performance Obligations
sections.content:
**Performance Obligation:** A promise to transfer a distinct good or service.

**Distinct if BOTH are met:**
1. **Capable of being distinct:** Customer can benefit from it on its own (or with readily available resources)
2. **Distinct within the contract:** Promise is separately identifiable from other promises

**Examples:**
• Software license + 1 year of updates = 2 POs (if updates are distinct)
• Equipment sale + installation = 1 PO (if installation is highly integrated)
• Product warranty = Separate PO if provides service beyond assurance

sections.title: Step 3: Determine Transaction Price
sections.content:
**Transaction Price:** The amount the entity expects to receive in exchange for transferring goods/services.

**Consider these factors:**

• **Variable consideration:** Discounts, rebates, refunds, bonuses
  - Use Expected Value (probability-weighted) OR Most Likely Amount
  - Apply **constraint:** Include only amounts NOT subject to significant reversal

• **Significant financing component:** Present value if payment timing > 1 year

• **Non-cash consideration:** Measure at fair value

• **Consideration payable to customer:** Reduce transaction price (rebates, coupons)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Variable Consideration Constraint
sections.content:
**The constraint is HEAVILY tested!**

Variable consideration is only included to the extent it is **HIGHLY PROBABLE** that a significant reversal will NOT occur.

**Factors indicating potential reversal:**
• Highly susceptible to factors outside entity's control
• Long time before uncertainty resolved
• Limited experience with similar contracts
• Practice of offering concessions
• Broad range of possible outcomes

**Example:** $1M contract with $200K bonus if delivered early. Only include bonus if highly probable you'll earn it.

sections.title: Step 4: Allocate Transaction Price
sections.content:
**Allocate based on Relative Standalone Selling Prices (SSP)**

**How to determine SSP:**
1. **Observable price:** Price when sold separately (best evidence)
2. **Adjusted market assessment:** What would market pay?
3. **Expected cost plus margin:** Your cost + reasonable margin
4. **Residual approach:** Only if SSP highly variable or uncertain

**Formula:**
Allocated Amount = (SSP of PO / Total SSP of all POs) × Transaction Price

sections.title: Step 5: Recognize Revenue
sections.content:
**Recognize when (or as) performance obligation is satisfied**

**Over Time Recognition (if ANY criterion met):**
1. Customer simultaneously receives and consumes benefits
2. Entity's performance creates/enhances an asset customer controls
3. Entity's performance creates an asset with no alternative use AND entity has enforceable right to payment

**Point in Time (if not over time):**
Recognize when control transfers to customer.

**Indicators of control transfer:**
• Entity has present right to payment
• Customer has legal title
• Physical possession transferred
• Customer has significant risks and rewards of ownership
• Customer has accepted the asset

sections.title: Measuring Progress (Over Time)
rows: When output directly measures value to customer
Efforts expended (costs incurred, labor hours, time elapsed)
When inputs correlate with transfer of control

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ASC 606 uses a 5-step model: Identify contract, POs, price, allocate, recognize
Performance obligation is distinct if customer can benefit alone AND separately identifiable
Variable consideration constrained to highly probable amounts
Allocate transaction price using relative standalone selling prices
Recognize over time if customer receives benefit continuously
Cost-to-cost is most common method for measuring progress
Control transfer indicators determine point-in-time recognition

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following conditions must ALL be met for a contract to exist under ASC 606?
options: Parties have approved the contract, each party's rights are identified, payment terms are identified, the contract has commercial substance, and collectibility of consideration is probable.
Parties have approved the contract, each party's rights are identified, payment terms are identified, and the contract has commercial substance.
Parties have approved the contract, payment terms are identified, the contract has commercial substance, and collectibility of consideration is probable.
Each party's rights are identified, payment terms are identified, the contract has commercial substance, and collectibility of consideration is probable.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under ASC 606, all five conditions (approval, rights, payment terms, commercial substance, and collectibility) must be met for a contract to exist and for revenue recognition to be appropriate. Missing any one of these elements means a contract, as defined by the standard, does not exist.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the five criteria for contract existence under ASC 606.

reveal.question: State the core principle of ASC 606 in your own words.
reveal.answer: Recognize revenue to depict the transfer of promised goods or services to customers in an amount that reflects the consideration the entity expects to be entitled to in exchange for those goods or services. (Essentially, recognize revenue when you've earned it by delivering what you promised.)

knowledgeCheck.question: A company sells a machine and offers a separate maintenance contract. Under ASC 606, the maintenance contract is considered a distinct performance obligation if:
options: The customer can benefit from the maintenance contract on its own or with readily available resources, and the promise to provide maintenance is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract.
The customer can benefit from the maintenance contract only if they purchase the machine, and the promise to provide maintenance is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract.

The customer can benefit from the maintenance contract on its own, regardless of readily available resources, and the promise to provide maintenance is not separately identifiable from other promises in the contract.
The customer can benefit from the maintenance contract only if they purchase the machine, and the promise to provide maintenance is not separately identifiable from other promises in the contract.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A performance obligation is distinct if the customer can benefit from it on its own (or with readily available resources) AND the promise is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract. This means the maintenance contract provides a separate benefit and is not inextricably linked to the machine itself.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about whether the customer gets value from the service independently and if the service is linked to other obligations.

title: Inventory Valuation Methods
description: Compare FIFO, LIFO, weighted average, and understand their financial statement impacts

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Inventory is often a company's LARGEST current asset. Your choice of valuation method directly impacts reported profits, taxes paid, and performance metrics. CMAs must understand these effects to advise on inventory policies and analyze competitor financials.

sections.title: Inventory Cost Flow Assumptions
rows: First costs In are First costs Out
Last costs In are First costs Out

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Rising Prices Effect
sections.content:
**"LIFO = Lower Income, Lower Inventory, Lower Taxes"**

In periods of RISING prices:
• **LIFO:** Higher COGS → Lower Net Income → Lower Taxes
• **FIFO:** Lower COGS → Higher Net Income → Higher Taxes

**Balance Sheet:**
• **LIFO:** Understated inventory (old costs)
• **FIFO:** Current inventory values (recent costs)

sections.title: FIFO (First-In, First-Out)
sections.content:
**Concept:** Oldest inventory costs flow to COGS first.

**Advantages:**
• Ending inventory reflects current replacement costs
• Matches physical flow for most businesses
• Permitted under GAAP AND IFRS

**Disadvantages:**
• During inflation: higher income = higher taxes
• May not match current costs with current revenues

**Example:**
Purchases: 100 @ $10, then 100 @ $12
Sold: 150 units
COGS = (100 × $10) + (50 × $12) = $1,600
Ending Inventory = 50 × $12 = $600

sections.title: LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)
sections.content:
**Concept:** Newest inventory costs flow to COGS first.

**Advantages:**
• Better matching of current costs with revenues
• Tax savings during inflation (higher COGS = lower taxable income)

**Disadvantages:**
• Inventory on B/S can be severely understated (old "layers")
• NOT permitted under IFRS
• LIFO conformity rule: If used for tax, must use for GAAP

**Example:**
Purchases: 100 @ $10, then 100 @ $12
Sold: 150 units
COGS = (100 × $12) + (50 × $10) = $1,700
Ending Inventory = 50 × $10 = $500

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: LIFO Liquidation
sections.content:
**LIFO Liquidation occurs when units sold > units purchased**

This dips into OLD, low-cost inventory layers, causing:
• Artificially LOW COGS
• Artificially HIGH income
• HIGHER taxes

**The opposite of why companies use LIFO!**

**Exam question pattern:** "Company using LIFO experienced unexpected sales surge..." → Watch for LIFO liquidation effects.

sections.title: Weighted Average Cost
sections.content:
**Concept:** All units have the same average cost.

**Calculation:**
Weighted Average Cost = Total Cost of Goods Available / Total Units Available

**Two variations:**
• **Periodic:** One average calculated at period end
• **Moving Average:** New average after each purchase

**Example (Periodic):**
Purchases: 100 @ $10 + 100 @ $12 = $2,200
Units available: 200
Average cost: $2,200 / 200 = $11/unit
COGS (150 sold): 150 × $11 = $1,650
Ending Inventory: 50 × $11 = $550

sections.title: Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value
sections.content:
**GAAP Requirement:** Inventory must be written down if NRV falls below cost.

**Net Realizable Value (NRV) =** Estimated selling price - Costs to complete and sell

**The test (for non-LIFO inventory):**
• Compare Cost vs. NRV
• Report at the LOWER amount
• Write-down recognized as loss in COGS

**For LIFO/Retail Method:**
Use Lower of Cost or Market (LCM)
• Market = Replacement cost (with ceiling and floor)
• Ceiling = NRV
• Floor = NRV - Normal profit margin

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FIFO: oldest costs to COGS, ending inventory reflects recent costs
LIFO: newest costs to COGS, tax advantage in inflation (US GAAP only)
Weighted average: smooths cost fluctuations
LIFO liquidation causes artificially high income - watch for this trap
Lower of cost or NRV required for inventory valuation
LIFO requires conformity: tax and book must match
IFRS prohibits LIFO - important for multinational comparisons

sections.title: FIFO vs. LIFO: Impact on Financial Statements

knowledgeCheck.question: During a period of rising prices, which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the impact of FIFO versus LIFO on a company's financial statements?
options: FIFO will result in a higher cost of goods sold and lower net income compared to LIFO.
FIFO will result in a lower cost of goods sold and higher net income compared to LIFO.
FIFO and LIFO will have the same impact on cost of goods sold and net income.
FIFO will result in a lower cost of goods sold and lower net income compared to LIFO.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: During inflation, FIFO assigns the older, cheaper costs to COGS, resulting in a lower COGS and therefore a higher net income. LIFO assigns the newer, more expensive costs to COGS, resulting in a higher COGS and lower net income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which costs (old or new) are being assigned to COGS under each method during a period of rising prices.

reveal.question: Is LIFO permitted under IFRS?
reveal.answer: No, LIFO is not permitted under International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS).

sections.title: FIFO vs. LIFO: A Quick Comparison

values: First-In, First-Out
Last-In, First-Out

values: Permitted

title: Long-Lived Assets and Depreciation
description: Master capitalization, depreciation methods, and impairment of PP&E and intangibles

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Capital expenditures are often a company's largest investments. CMAs make capitalization decisions, choose depreciation methods affecting reported income, and test for impairment. These decisions directly impact performance metrics and capital budgeting analysis.

sections.title: Capitalize vs. Expense Decision
sections.content:
**Capitalize (add to asset) when:**
• Expenditure provides future economic benefit
• Extends useful life OR increases productivity
• Initial acquisition or construction costs

**Expense immediately when:**
• Maintains current operating capacity
• Repairs and routine maintenance
• Does not extend life or add value

**Capitalized Costs for PP&E:**
• Purchase price (less discounts)
• Import duties and non-refundable taxes
• Directly attributable costs to bring asset to intended use
• Site preparation, delivery, installation, testing
• Professional fees (architects, legal for construction)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capitalization Test
sections.content:
**"FUEL"** - Capitalize if it provides:

**F**uture economic benefit
**U**seful life extended
**E**fficiency or capacity increased
**L**egal requirement for operation

If it just maintains existing capacity → EXPENSE IT

sections.title: Straight-Line Depreciation
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Annual Depreciation = (Cost - Salvage Value) / Useful Life

**Example:**
Machine cost: $100,000
Salvage value: $10,000
Useful life: 5 years

Annual depreciation = ($100,000 - $10,000) / 5 = $18,000/year

**Advantages:**
• Simple to calculate
• Consistent expense each period
• Matches assets with even usage patterns

sections.title: Double-Declining Balance (DDB)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Depreciation = (2 / Useful Life) × Beginning Book Value

**Key Rules:**
• IGNORE salvage value in calculation (but never depreciate below salvage)
• Switch to straight-line when it gives higher depreciation

**Example (Year 1):**
Cost: $100,000, Life: 5 years, Salvage: $10,000
Rate: 2/5 = 40%
Year 1: $100,000 × 40% = $40,000
Year 2: $60,000 × 40% = $24,000
Year 3: $36,000 × 40% = $14,400
(Continue until book value = salvage value)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Partial Year Depreciation
sections.content:
**Watch for assets acquired mid-year!**

**Common conventions:**
• **Half-year:** 6 months depreciation in year of acquisition
• **Full-month:** Depreciation from month placed in service
• **Nearest month:** Round to nearest whole month

**Example:** Asset purchased October 1, straight-line $12,000/year
• Full year: $12,000
• Half-year: $6,000
• Full-month: $12,000 × 3/12 = $3,000

sections.title: Impairment of Long-Lived Assets
sections.content:
**Two-Step Test (GAAP):**

**Step 1: Recoverability Test**
Is carrying value > undiscounted future cash flows?
• If YES → Asset is impaired, go to Step 2
• If NO → No impairment

**Step 2: Measurement**
Impairment Loss = Carrying Value - Fair Value

**When to test:**
• Significant decrease in market value
• Adverse change in use or physical condition
• Adverse change in legal or business climate
• Operating losses or negative cash flows

**Impairment losses are NOT reversible under GAAP!**

sections.title: Intangible Assets
sections.content:
**Identifiable intangibles:** Patents, copyrights, trademarks, franchises, customer lists

**Finite life intangibles:**
• Amortize over useful life (straight-line typical)
• Subject to impairment testing
• Examples: Patents (legal life 20 years), copyrights

**Indefinite life intangibles:**
• NO amortization
• Annual impairment test required
• Example: Trademarks (can be renewed indefinitely)

**Goodwill:**
• Excess of purchase price over fair value of net assets acquired
• NOT amortizable
• Annual impairment test at reporting unit level

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capitalize costs providing future benefit; expense maintenance
Straight-line: equal annual amounts, most common method
DDB: ignore salvage in calculation, accelerated pattern
Partial year depreciation - watch for mid-year purchases
Impairment: two-step test using undiscounted then fair value
Impairment losses cannot be reversed under GAAP
Indefinite-life intangibles and goodwill are NOT amortized

sections.title: Capitalization vs. Expense: Test Your Understanding

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following costs related to a company's machinery should be expensed immediately, rather than capitalized?
options: A major overhaul that extends the machine's useful life by three years.
The initial cost of installing the machinery.
A routine oil change to maintain the machine's operating condition.
An upgrade that increases the machine's production output by 20%.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Routine maintenance, such as an oil change, keeps the asset in its current operating condition and does not provide future economic benefit beyond the current period. Therefore, it should be expensed immediately. Overhauls, installation costs, and upgrades that increase productivity or extend useful life should be capitalized.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about whether the expenditure provides a future economic benefit or simply maintains the asset's current condition.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating annual depreciation expense using the straight-line method?
reveal.answer: Annual Depreciation = (Cost - Salvage Value) / Useful Life

knowledgeCheck.question: A company purchases a delivery truck for $60,000. The estimated salvage value is $5,000, and the useful life is 10 years. What is the annual depreciation expense using the straight-line method?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Using the straight-line depreciation formula: ($60,000 - $5,000) / 10 = $5,500. Therefore, the annual depreciation expense is $5,500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to subtract the salvage value from the cost before dividing by the useful life.

title: Leases (ASC 842)
description: Apply the new lease accounting standard for lessees and lessors

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: ASC 842 brought nearly all leases onto the balance sheet, significantly impacting debt ratios, EBITDA, and performance metrics. CMAs must understand lease classification, measurement, and the strategic implications of lease vs. buy decisions.

sections.title: Core Principle of ASC 842
sections.content:
**Lessee Recognition:**
Virtually ALL leases create a Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset and a Lease Liability on the balance sheet.

**Exception:** Short-term leases (≤12 months, no purchase option reasonably certain to exercise) may be expensed straight-line.

**Major Change from Old Rules:**
Previously, operating leases were "off-balance sheet." Now they're ON the balance sheet, affecting leverage ratios.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Finance vs. Operating Lease
sections.content:
**"OWNES"** - Any ONE criterion = Finance Lease:

**O**wnership transfers at end of lease
**W**ritten purchase option reasonably certain to exercise
**N**inety percent (≥90%) of fair value is present value of payments
**E**conomic life: Lease term is ≥75% of remaining economic life
**S**pecialized: Asset is specialized with no alternative use to lessor

None of these? → **Operating Lease**

sections.title: Lessee Accounting: Initial Measurement
sections.content:
**Lease Liability =** Present value of lease payments

**Lease payments include:**
• Fixed payments (less any lease incentives)
• Variable payments based on index or rate
• Exercise price of purchase option (if reasonably certain)
• Penalties for termination (if lease term reflects termination)
• Residual value guarantees

**ROU Asset =** Lease liability
+ Initial direct costs
+ Prepaid lease payments
- Lease incentives received

**Discount Rate:**
• Rate implicit in the lease (if readily determinable)
• Lessee's incremental borrowing rate (if implicit rate not known)

sections.title: Lessee Accounting: Finance Lease
sections.content:
**Subsequent Measurement:**

**ROU Asset:**
• Amortize (typically straight-line over lease term)
• Test for impairment

**Lease Liability:**
• Reduce by payments made
• Increase by interest accrual (effective interest method)

**Income Statement Impact:**
• Amortization expense (separate line)
• Interest expense (separate line)
• **Front-loaded expense pattern** (like owning the asset)

sections.title: Lessee Accounting: Operating Lease
sections.content:
**Subsequent Measurement:**

**ROU Asset:**
• Calculated as a "plug" to achieve straight-line lease expense
• Not separately amortized

**Lease Liability:**
• Reduce by payments made
• Increase by interest accrual

**Income Statement Impact:**
• Single "lease expense" (straight-line over lease term)
• Shown within operating expenses
• **Even expense pattern** (like renting)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Interest Pattern
sections.content:
**Both lease types use effective interest on the liability!**

But total expense pattern differs:

**Finance Lease:**
• High interest early (larger liability balance)
• Steady amortization
• Total expense = Interest + Amortization
• **Result: Higher expense in early years**

**Operating Lease:**
• Interest is front-loaded
• BUT ROU Asset reduction balances it out
• **Result: Straight-line TOTAL expense**

sections.title: Comparison: Finance vs. Operating Lease
rows: Principal in Financing, Interest in Operating

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ASC 842: Nearly all leases go on balance sheet as ROU Asset and Lease Liability
OWNES criteria (any one) = Finance Lease; none = Operating Lease
Lease liability = PV of lease payments at implicit rate or IBR
ROU Asset = Liability + Initial costs + Prepayments - Incentives
Finance lease: front-loaded total expense (interest + amortization)
Operating lease: straight-line total expense
Short-term lease exception (≤12 months) allows off-balance sheet treatment

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following factors, if present in a lease agreement, would classify the lease as a finance lease under ASC 842?
options: The lease term is for 75% or more of the remaining economic life of the underlying asset.
The present value of the lease payments equals or substantially exceeds all of the fair value of the underlying asset.
The lease transfers ownership of the asset to the lessee by the end of the lease term.
All of the above.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under ASC 842, a lease is classified as a finance lease if any of the following criteria are met: (1) the lease transfers ownership of the asset to the lessee by the end of the lease term; (2) the lessee has an option to purchase the asset that the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise; (3) the lease term is for the major part of the remaining economic life of the underlying asset (75% or more); or (4) the present value of the sum of the lease payments and any residual value guaranteed by the lessee equals or exceeds substantially all of the fair value of the underlying asset.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the criteria for classifying a lease as a finance lease under ASC 842.

reveal.question: List five components included in the calculation of the lease liability.
reveal.answer: The five components included in the calculation of the lease liability are: Fixed payments (less any lease incentives), Variable payments based on an index or rate, Exercise price of a purchase option (if reasonably certain), Penalties for termination (if the lease term reflects the termination option being exercised), and Amounts probable of being owed by the lessee under a residual value guarantee.

sections.title: Finance Lease vs. Operating Lease: Income Statement Impact

rows.label: Expense Recognition
values: Amortization Expense (ROU Asset) and Interest Expense (Lease Liability), presented separately
Single Lease Expense (including both interest and amortization), presented on one line

rows.label: Expense Pattern
values: Generally, higher expense in early years (due to interest expense) and lower expense in later years
Straight-line expense over the lease term (usually)

rows.label: Cash Flow Classification
values: Principal portion of lease payments is a financing activity; interest portion is an operating activity.
Operating activity

rows.label: Profitability
values: Lower profitability in early years due to higher expense.
Smoother impact on profitability.

title: Income Taxes (ASC 740)
description: Understand deferred tax assets, liabilities, and valuation allowances

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Income tax accounting affects net income, effective tax rates, and balance sheet presentation. CMAs must understand why book and tax income differ, how to calculate deferred taxes, and when a valuation allowance is required. Tax provision work is a key CMA competency.

sections.title: Book vs. Tax Differences
sections.content:
**Why do book and tax income differ?**
GAAP and tax law have different objectives:
• GAAP: Match revenues and expenses, fair presentation
• Tax: Generate revenue for government, encourage certain behaviors

**Two types of differences:**

**Temporary Differences:**
• Will reverse in future periods
• Create deferred tax assets or liabilities
• Example: Accelerated depreciation for tax

**Permanent Differences:**
• Will NEVER reverse
• Affect effective tax rate only
• Example: Municipal bond interest (book income, never taxed)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DTL vs. DTA
sections.content:
**"Future Tax Bill"** approach:

**Deferred Tax LIABILITY (DTL):**
• You owe ADDITIONAL taxes in the future
• Book income > Tax income NOW
• Example: Depreciation faster for tax than book

**Deferred Tax ASSET (DTA):**
• You'll pay LESS taxes in the future
• Tax income > Book income NOW
• Example: Warranty expense accrued on books, deducted when paid for tax

sections.title: Calculating Deferred Taxes
sections.content:
**The Balance Sheet Approach (ASC 740):**

**Step 1:** Identify temporary differences (book vs. tax basis of assets/liabilities)

**Step 2:** Classify as future taxable or deductible amounts

**Step 3:** Apply enacted tax rate

**Formula:**
Deferred Tax = Temporary Difference × Tax Rate

**Example:**
Equipment: Book basis $80,000, Tax basis $60,000
Difference: $20,000 (will be taxable in future as less depreciation available)
Tax rate: 21%
DTL = $20,000 × 21% = $4,200

sections.title: Common Temporary Differences
rows: Lower tax basis → future taxable amount
Deducted when written off for tax
Taxed when received, book income later
Book revenue now, taxed when collected
Book income now, taxed when sold

sections.title: Valuation Allowance
sections.content:
**When Required:**
Reduce DTA by a valuation allowance if it is **more likely than not** (>50% probability) that some or all of the DTA will NOT be realized.

**Evidence to Consider:**

**Negative (suggesting need for allowance):**
• History of operating losses
• Losses expected in future
• Carryforwards expiring unused

**Positive (may avoid allowance):**
• Existing taxable temporary differences reversing
• Future taxable income from existing contracts
• Tax planning strategies available

**Journal Entry:**
Dr. Income Tax Expense (or OCI)
  Cr. Valuation Allowance (contra to DTA)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Rate Changes
sections.content:
**Deferred taxes are measured at ENACTED future rates!**

If tax rates change:
• Remeasure ALL deferred taxes at new rate
• Recognize adjustment in income tax expense

**Example:**
DTL of $10,000 at 35% rate
New rate enacted: 21%
New DTL = ($10,000 / 0.35) × 0.21 = $6,000
Benefit recognized: $4,000 reduction in tax expense

**Watch for:** Questions with "enacted" vs. "proposed" rates - only ENACTED matters!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Temporary differences reverse; permanent differences never do
DTL = future taxable amount (book > tax now)
DTA = future deductible amount (tax > book now)
Use balance sheet approach: compare book and tax basis
Valuation allowance if more-likely-than-not DTA won't be realized
Use ENACTED tax rates for measurement
Rate changes require remeasurement through income tax expense

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements best describes the primary difference between GAAP and tax accounting?
options: GAAP focuses on generating revenue for the government, while tax accounting focuses on matching revenues and expenses.
GAAP aims for a fair presentation of financial results, while tax accounting is designed to generate revenue for the government and incentivize specific behaviors.
GAAP and tax accounting have the same objectives, but use different methods for calculating income.
GAAP is required for all companies, while tax accounting is optional.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) prioritizes the accurate and fair presentation of a company's financial position and results of operations. Tax accounting, on the other hand, is primarily concerned with determining the amount of tax owed to the government and may include incentives for certain activities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the purpose of each accounting framework.

reveal.question: Define a temporary difference in the context of deferred taxes.
reveal.answer: A temporary difference is a difference between the book (GAAP) basis of an asset or liability and its tax basis that will result in taxable or deductible amounts in future years when the asset is recovered or the liability is settled.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a temporary difference of $50,000 related to accelerated depreciation for tax purposes. This difference will result in future taxable income. If the enacted tax rate is 25%, what is the amount of the deferred tax liability?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The deferred tax liability is calculated as the temporary difference multiplied by the enacted tax rate. In this case, $50,000 * 25% = $12,500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Deferred Tax = Temporary Difference × Tax Rate

title: Stockholders' Equity Transactions
description: Account for stock issuances, treasury stock, dividends, and stock splits

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: CMAs advise on capital structure decisions, dividend policies, and stock compensation. Understanding equity transactions is essential for financial analysis, EPS calculations, and communicating with investors about capital allocation strategies.

sections.title: Components of Stockholders' Equity
sections.content:
**Contributed Capital:**
• **Common Stock:** Par value × shares issued
• **Preferred Stock:** Par value × shares issued
• **Additional Paid-in Capital (APIC):** Amount received above par

**Earned Capital:**
• **Retained Earnings:** Cumulative net income less dividends

**Other:**
• **Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (AOCI):** OCI items not in net income
• **Treasury Stock:** Cost of repurchased shares (contra-equity)

sections.title: Stock Issuance
sections.content:
**Issuance at par:**
Dr. Cash (par × shares)
  Cr. Common Stock (par × shares)

**Issuance above par:**
Dr. Cash (issue price × shares)
  Cr. Common Stock (par × shares)
  Cr. APIC - Common Stock (excess)

**Example:**
Issue 1,000 shares, $1 par, at $15 per share
Dr. Cash $15,000
  Cr. Common Stock $1,000
  Cr. APIC $14,000

**No-par stock:** All proceeds to Common Stock (or designate stated value)

sections.title: Treasury Stock: Cost Method
sections.content:
**Cost Method (most common):**

**Acquisition:**
Dr. Treasury Stock (cost)
  Cr. Cash

**Reissuance above cost:**
Dr. Cash
  Cr. Treasury Stock (cost)
  Cr. APIC - Treasury Stock (gain)

**Reissuance below cost:**
Dr. Cash
Dr. APIC - Treasury Stock (to extent available)
Dr. Retained Earnings (if APIC exhausted)
  Cr. Treasury Stock (cost)

**Key:** Never recognize gain/loss on own stock transactions!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Dividend Dates
sections.content:
**"DERP"** - Four key dividend dates:

**D**eclaration Date - Board declares dividend (liability created)
**E**x-dividend Date - Stock trades without dividend (set by exchange)
**R**ecord Date - Shareholders on record receive dividend
**P**ayment Date - Cash actually paid

**Journal entries only on Declaration and Payment dates!**

sections.title: Cash Dividends
sections.content:
**Declaration Date:**
Dr. Retained Earnings (or Dividends Declared)
  Cr. Dividends Payable

**Payment Date:**
Dr. Dividends Payable
  Cr. Cash

**Dividend Calculation:**
Total Dividend = Dividend per share × Shares outstanding

**Watch for preferred dividends:**
• Cumulative preferred: Must pay ALL past and current before common
• Non-cumulative: Only current year's dividends required
• Participating: May share in additional dividends with common

sections.title: Stock Dividends vs. Stock Splits
rows: New shares issued to existing holders

sections.title: Stock Dividend Accounting
sections.content:
**Small Stock Dividend (< 20-25%):**
Dr. Retained Earnings (FMV × new shares)
  Cr. Common Stock (par × new shares)
  Cr. APIC (excess of FMV over par)

**Large Stock Dividend (≥ 20-25%):**
Dr. Retained Earnings (par × new shares)
  Cr. Common Stock (par × new shares)

**Example (Small):**
10% dividend on 10,000 shares, $1 par, $25 FMV
New shares: 1,000
Dr. Retained Earnings $25,000
  Cr. Common Stock $1,000
  Cr. APIC $24,000

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Stock issuance: Common Stock at par, excess to APIC
Treasury stock reduces total equity (contra-equity account)
Cost method: Record treasury stock at acquisition cost
No gains/losses recognized on treasury stock transactions
Dividend dates: Declaration (liability), Payment (cash outflow)
Small stock dividends: FMV transferred from Retained Earnings
Stock splits: Memo entry only, par value decreases proportionally

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. issues 5,000 shares of its $2 par value common stock for $30 per share. What is the journal entry to record this transaction?
options: Dr. Cash $150,000; Cr. Common Stock $10,000; Cr. APIC - Common Stock $140,000
Dr. Cash $10,000; Cr. Common Stock $10,000
Dr. Cash $150,000; Cr. Common Stock $150,000
Dr. Cash $140,000; Cr. Common Stock $10,000; Cr. APIC - Common Stock $130,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct entry is: Dr. Cash $150,000 (5,000 shares * $30), Cr. Common Stock $10,000 (5,000 shares * $2 par), Cr. APIC - Common Stock $140,000 (5,000 shares * ($30 - $2)). APIC represents the amount received above the par value.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to separate the par value from the excess paid-in capital.

reveal.question: What is the journal entry to record the acquisition of treasury stock using the cost method?
reveal.answer: The journal entry is: Dr. Treasury Stock (at cost); Cr. Cash. Treasury stock is recorded at the price paid to reacquire the shares.

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Company reacquired 1,000 shares of its own stock for $25 per share. Later, it reissues these shares for $30 per share. Which of the following is the correct entry to record the reissuance?
options: Dr. Cash $30,000; Cr. Treasury Stock $25,000; Cr. Retained Earnings $5,000
Dr. Cash $30,000; Cr. Treasury Stock $30,000
Dr. Cash $30,000; Cr. Treasury Stock $25,000; Cr. APIC - Treasury Stock $5,000
Dr. Cash $25,000; Dr. APIC - Treasury Stock $5,000; Cr. Treasury Stock $30,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct entry is Dr. Cash $30,000 (1,000 * $30); Cr. Treasury Stock $25,000 (1,000 * $25 - original cost); Cr. APIC - Treasury Stock $5,000 (1,000 * $5 - the difference between the reissuance price and the cost).  Gains on treasury stock transactions are credited to APIC-Treasury Stock, never Retained Earnings.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Gains on treasury stock are never recorded as income.  APIC-Treasury Stock is used.

title: SEC Reporting and FASB Framework
description: Understand SEC filing requirements and the FASB conceptual framework

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: CMAs working for public companies must understand SEC filing requirements and deadlines. The FASB conceptual framework guides accounting policy choices when specific standards don't address an issue. This knowledge is essential for financial reporting leadership roles.

sections.title: Filer Categories and Deadlines
sections.content:
**Large Accelerated Filer:** Public float ≥ $700M
• 10-K: 60 days
• 10-Q: 40 days

**Accelerated Filer:** Public float $75M - $700M
• 10-K: 75 days
• 10-Q: 40 days

**Non-accelerated Filer:** Public float < $75M
• 10-K: 90 days
• 10-Q: 45 days

**Public float = market value of shares held by non-affiliates**

sections.title: FASB Conceptual Framework
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Guides development of accounting standards and helps resolve issues not addressed by specific standards.

**Objective of Financial Reporting:**
Provide information useful to existing and potential investors, lenders, and other creditors in making decisions about providing resources.

**Primary Users:** Investors and creditors (capital providers)

sections.title: Qualitative Characteristics
sections.content:
**Fundamental Characteristics:**

**Relevance:**
• Predictive value - helps predict future
• Confirmatory value - confirms past predictions
• Materiality - could influence decisions

**Faithful Representation:**
• Complete - all necessary information
• Neutral - unbiased
• Free from error - no mistakes in process

**Enhancing Characteristics:**
• Comparability (across companies)
• Verifiability (others can agree)
• Timeliness (available when needed)
• Understandability (clear presentation)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Faithful Representation
sections.content:
**"CNF"** - Faithful Representation requires:

**C**omplete - Nothing missing that could affect decisions
**N**eutral - No bias toward particular outcome  
**F**ree from error - Process was carefully applied

**Note:** "Free from error" doesn't mean perfectly accurate - estimates are allowed!

sections.title: GAAP Hierarchy
sections.content:
**When specific guidance exists:**

**Category A (Authoritative):**
• FASB Accounting Standards Codification (ASC)
• SEC rules and interpretive releases (for SEC registrants)

**When no specific guidance:**
• Consider similar transactions in the Codification
• Apply conceptual framework
• May consider non-authoritative guidance (AICPA, IFRS)

**The Codification is the single source of authoritative GAAP!**

sections.title: Elements of Financial Statements
rows: Probable future economic benefits controlled by entity
Probable future sacrifices from past transactions
Increases in equity from peripheral transactions
Decreases in equity from peripheral transactions

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 10-K (annual), 10-Q (quarterly), 8-K (current events) are key SEC filings
Filing deadlines depend on filer category (based on public float)
Conceptual framework: Relevance and Faithful Representation are fundamental
Faithful Representation = Complete, Neutral, Free from Error
FASB ASC is the single source of authoritative GAAP
Conceptual framework used when specific guidance doesn't exist
Five elements: Assets, Liabilities, Equity, Revenues, Expenses (plus Gains/Losses)

knowledgeCheck.question: Apex Corp. has a public float of $650 million. What are the deadlines for filing its 10-K and 10-Q reports, respectively?
options: 60 days and 40 days
75 days and 40 days
90 days and 45 days
75 days and 45 days
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Apex Corp., with a public float of $650 million, is classified as an Accelerated Filer. Accelerated Filers have a 75-day deadline for the 10-K and a 40-day deadline for the 10-Q.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the public float thresholds for each filer category.

reveal.question: What is the primary objective of financial reporting according to the FASB Conceptual Framework?
reveal.answer: To provide financial information about the reporting entity that is useful to existing and potential investors, lenders, and other creditors in making decisions about providing resources to the entity. Those decisions involve buying, selling, or holding equity and debt instruments, and providing or settling loans and other forms of credit.

rows.label: Definition
values: Information capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users.
Information that is complete, neutral, and free from material error.

rows.label: Components
values: Predictive Value, Confirmatory Value, Materiality
Completeness, Neutrality, Free from Error

title: Financial Instruments and Investments
description: Classify financial assets, apply the equity method, and navigate the fair value hierarchy

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Companies hold billions in financial assets — from short-term bond portfolios to strategic equity stakes. As a CMA, you must know how classification drives income statement volatility and balance sheet presentation. A single reclassification can swing reported earnings by millions. The CMA exam tests this area heavily because misclassifying investments is one of the most common financial reporting errors.

sections.title: Classification of Debt Securities (ASC 320)
rows: Positive intent AND ability to hold to maturity

sections.title: Equity Investments (ASC 321)
sections.content:
**Equity securities with readily determinable fair values:**
• Measured at **fair value through net income (FV-NI)**
• ALL unrealized gains/losses flow through the income statement
• No OCI option for equity securities (unlike debt AFS)

**Equity securities WITHOUT readily determinable fair values:**
• May elect **measurement alternative:** Cost minus impairment ± observable price changes
• This avoids the volatility of full fair value measurement

**Key Change from Old GAAP:**
The old "available-for-sale" category for equities is gone. Unrealized gains/losses on equity securities now always hit net income (except for the measurement alternative).

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Investment Classification
sections.content:
**"Where do unrealized gains/losses go?"**

Think **"TIN-OCA":**

**T**rading → **I**ncome statement (**N**et Income)
**O**CI → Available-for-Sale debt (bypasses income statement)
**C**ost → Held-to-Maturity (no gain/loss recognized)
**A**ll equity securities → Net Income (under ASC 321)

**Ownership Percentage Guide:**
• **< 20%** → Fair value (ASC 321) — little influence
• **20-50%** → Equity method (ASC 323) — significant influence
• **> 50%** → Consolidation — control

sections.title: Equity Method Investments (20-50% Ownership)
sections.content:
**When to Apply:** Investor has **significant influence** over investee (presumed at 20-50% ownership).

**Initial Recognition:**
Dr. Investment in Investee    XXX
  Cr. Cash                       XXX

**Subsequent Measurement:**

**Investee reports net income of $100,000 (investor owns 30%):**
Dr. Investment in Investee    30,000
  Cr. Equity in Earnings of Investee    30,000

**Investee pays dividends of $40,000:**
Dr. Cash (30% × $40,000)    12,000
  Cr. Investment in Investee    12,000

**Critical Concept:** Dividends REDUCE the investment balance — they are NOT income! Income is recognized as the investor's share of the investee's earnings.

**Investment Balance = Cost + Share of Income − Share of Dividends − Amortization of Basis Differences**

sections.title: Fair Value Hierarchy (ASC 820)
sections.content:
**Level 1 — Quoted Prices in Active Markets**
• Most reliable: stock prices on NYSE, bond prices on active exchanges
• Use unadjusted quoted prices

**Level 2 — Observable Inputs (Other Than Level 1)**
• Quoted prices in inactive markets
• Quoted prices for similar assets/liabilities
• Observable interest rates, yield curves
• Example: Corporate bond priced using comparable bond yields

**Level 3 — Unobservable Inputs**
• Least reliable: entity's own assumptions
• Used when market data is unavailable
• Requires most extensive disclosures
• Example: Valuing a private equity investment using DCF model

**Hierarchy Rule:** Use the HIGHEST level of input available. You cannot choose Level 3 when Level 1 data exists.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Impairment and Reclassification
sections.content:
**Impairment of Debt Securities (AFS and HTM):**
• Use the **Current Expected Credit Loss (CECL)** model under ASC 326
• Credit-related impairment → Allowance (income statement loss)
• Non-credit-related impairment on AFS → OCI

**Impairment of Equity Method Investments:**
• If decline is **other than temporary** → Write down to fair value
• Loss recognized in income statement (not recoverable)

**Common Exam Traps:**
1. Trading securities are ALWAYS at fair value through NI — no impairment test needed (already marked to market)
2. HTM securities are at amortized cost — but still tested for credit losses under CECL
3. Selling HTM securities "taints" the entire portfolio — may force reclassification
4. AFS → Trading reclassification: unrealized gain/loss already in OCI stays in OCI

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Debt securities: HTM (amortized cost), Trading (FV-NI), AFS (FV-OCI)
Equity securities generally measured at FV through net income under ASC 321
Equity method (20-50%): Investment = Cost + Share of Income − Dividends
Dividends received under equity method reduce the investment, NOT income
Fair value hierarchy: Level 1 (quoted prices) > Level 2 (observable) > Level 3 (unobservable)
CECL model applies to debt security impairment; equity method uses other-than-temporary test
Trading securities never need separate impairment testing — already at fair value

sections.title: Equity Securities Fair Value Through Net Income (FV-NI)

knowledgeCheck.question: Company A holds an equity security with a readily determinable fair value. During the year, the security's fair value increased by $50,000. How is this increase reported in Company A's financial statements?
options: As an unrealized gain in Other Comprehensive Income (OCI).
As an unrealized gain in the Income Statement.
As a direct increase to Retained Earnings.
No gain is recognized until the security is sold.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Equity securities with readily determinable fair values are measured at fair value through net income (FV-NI). This means that all unrealized gains and losses are recognized in the income statement in the period they occur.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the treatment for equity securities is different than debt securities classified as Available-for-Sale (AFS).

reveal.question: What level of ownership generally indicates that an investor has significant influence over an investee, requiring the use of the equity method?
reveal.answer: Ownership between 20% and 50% typically indicates significant influence, triggering the use of the equity method. This is a rebuttable presumption, meaning other factors can influence the decision.

sections.title: Equity Method Investment - Net Income Reporting

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X owns 40% of Company Y and uses the equity method. Company Y reports net income of $200,000 for the year. What is the journal entry Company X will make to record its share of Company Y's net income?
options: Dr. Investment in Company Y $80,000; Cr. Cash $80,000
Dr. Investment in Company Y $80,000; Cr. Equity Income $80,000
Dr. Equity Income $80,000; Cr. Investment in Company Y $80,000
No journal entry is required until Company Y pays a dividend.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under the equity method, the investor recognizes its share of the investee's net income by debiting the Investment in Investee account and crediting Equity Income. The calculation is 40% * $200,000 = $80,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember, the equity method adjusts the investment account based on the investee's earnings, not dividends.

title: Business Combinations
description: Apply the acquisition method under ASC 805 for business combinations, goodwill, and non-controlling interests

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Mergers and acquisitions reshape entire organizations. CMAs must understand how to measure consideration transferred, allocate the purchase price to identifiable assets and liabilities, calculate goodwill, and test it for impairment. These transactions generate some of the largest line items on the balance sheet and are a perennial CMA exam favorite.

sections.title: The Acquisition Method (ASC 805)
sections.content:
**ASC 805 requires the acquisition method for ALL business combinations.**

**Four Steps:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Acquirer**
• The entity that obtains control (usually > 50% voting interest)
• In a stock-for-stock deal, the acquirer is the entity whose shareholders control the combined entity

**Step 2 — Determine the Acquisition Date**
• The date the acquirer obtains control
• Usually the closing date of the transaction

**Step 3 — Measure the Consideration Transferred**
• Cash paid
• Fair value of equity instruments issued
• Fair value of any contingent consideration
• **NOT included:** Acquisition-related costs (legal, advisory fees → expense as incurred)

**Step 4 — Recognize and Measure Identifiable Assets Acquired and Liabilities Assumed**
• Measured at **acquisition-date fair values**
• Includes previously unrecognized intangibles (customer lists, brand names, patents)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Goodwill Calculation
sections.content:
**"Goodwill is what you OVERPAID"**

**Goodwill = Consideration Transferred − Fair Value of Net Identifiable Assets**

Or equivalently:
**Goodwill = Purchase Price − (FV of Assets − FV of Liabilities)**

**Example:**
• Purchase price: $5,000,000
• FV of identifiable assets: $6,000,000
• FV of liabilities assumed: $2,500,000
• FV of net assets: $3,500,000
• **Goodwill = $5,000,000 − $3,500,000 = $1,500,000**

**Remember:** If the net assets are worth MORE than you paid → **Bargain Purchase Gain** (recognized immediately in income statement).

sections.title: Identifiable Intangible Assets
rows: Customer lists, customer relationships, order backlog

sections.title: Goodwill Impairment Testing
sections.content:
**Goodwill is NOT amortized — it is tested for impairment at least annually.**

**Reporting Unit Level:**
Goodwill is assigned to reporting units (operating segments or components).

**Optional Qualitative Assessment (Step 0):**
• Assess whether it is **more likely than not** (>50%) that fair value of reporting unit < carrying amount
• If not likely → No further testing needed
• Factors: macroeconomic, industry, entity-specific, reporting unit events

**Quantitative Test (if Step 0 fails or is skipped):**
• Compare **fair value of reporting unit** to its **carrying amount** (including goodwill)
• If carrying amount > fair value → **Impairment loss** = excess (but limited to goodwill balance)
• Write down goodwill; loss recognized in income statement
• **One-step test** (simplified in 2017 — no more "Step 2" hypothetical purchase price allocation)

**Impairment losses on goodwill are NEVER reversed!**

sections.title: Bargain Purchase and Non-Controlling Interests
sections.content:
**Bargain Purchase (Negative Goodwill):**
• Occurs when FV of net assets > consideration transferred
• Before recognizing a gain, reassess: Are all assets and liabilities properly identified and measured?
• If confirmed → Recognize **gain in income statement** on acquisition date
• This is rare and should raise red flags

**Non-Controlling Interest (NCI):**
• Arises when acquirer obtains < 100% of target (e.g., buys 80%)
• NCI measured at either:
  - **Fair value** (full goodwill method) — preferred under ASC
  - **Proportionate share of net assets** (partial goodwill method)

**Full Goodwill Example:**
• Purchase 80% for $4,000,000
• FV of 100% = $5,000,000
• NCI (20%) = $1,000,000
• Total goodwill = $5,000,000 − $3,500,000 net assets = $1,500,000

**NCI is reported in equity section of consolidated balance sheet, separate from parent equity.**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Acquisition Costs and Contingent Consideration
sections.content:
**Acquisition-Related Costs:**
• Legal fees, due diligence, advisory fees → **EXPENSE as incurred**
• They are NOT part of the purchase price
• Stock issuance costs → Reduce APIC (contra-equity)

**Contingent Consideration (Earnouts):**
• Measured at fair value on acquisition date
• Classified as liability or equity
• If liability: remeasured each period with changes in **income statement**
• If equity: NOT remeasured

**Common Exam Tricks:**
1. Including advisory fees in goodwill calculation (WRONG — expense them)
2. Forgetting to include contingent consideration in purchase price
3. Using book values instead of fair values for acquired assets
4. Amortizing goodwill (goodwill is NOT amortized under US GAAP — only tested for impairment)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Acquisition method is required for all business combinations under ASC 805
Goodwill = Consideration transferred − FV of net identifiable assets acquired
Acquired assets and liabilities measured at acquisition-date fair values
Goodwill is not amortized — tested for impairment annually (quantitative or qualitative)
Impairment = Carrying amount of reporting unit − Fair value (capped at goodwill balance)
Acquisition-related costs are expensed, NOT capitalized into goodwill
Non-controlling interest reported in equity; measured at fair value or proportionate share
Bargain purchase gain recognized immediately in income statement after reassessment

knowledgeCheck.question: Company A acquires 60% of the voting stock of Company B in exchange for cash. Company A's shareholders will continue to control the combined entity. Under ASC 805, which company is considered the acquirer?
options: Company A
Company B
The combined entity
It cannot be determined from the information given.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: ASC 805 states that the acquirer is the entity that obtains control. In this case, Company A acquired 60% of Company B's voting stock, thus giving Company A control.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which company gains control of the other.

reveal.question: How often is goodwill tested for impairment?
reveal.answer: Goodwill is tested for impairment at least annually. It is NOT amortized.

knowledgeCheck.question: Before performing a quantitative goodwill impairment test, an entity may elect to perform a qualitative assessment (Step 0). What is the threshold for proceeding to the quantitative test?
options: Fair value is less than carrying amount.
It is more likely than not that the fair value of the reporting unit is less than its carrying amount.
Fair value is greater than the carrying amount.
It is reasonably possible that the fair value of the reporting unit is less than its carrying amount.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The qualitative assessment (Step 0) determines if it is more likely than not (>50%) that the fair value of the reporting unit is less than its carrying amount. If so, a quantitative test is required.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the 'more likely than not' threshold.

title: Employee Benefits and Pensions
description: Account for defined benefit pension plans, OPEB obligations, and pension cost components under ASC 715

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Pension and post-retirement benefit obligations can dwarf a company's market capitalization — just ask General Motors or Boeing. CMAs must understand how defined benefit plans create massive liabilities (or assets) on the balance sheet and how the components of pension cost ripple through the income statement and OCI. This is one of the most calculation-heavy topics on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Defined Contribution vs. Defined Benefit Plans
rows: Traditional pension paying 60% of final salary

sections.title: Components of Net Periodic Pension Cost (ASC 715)
sections.content:
**Net periodic pension cost has several components — only ONE goes to operating expense:**

**In Income Statement (Operating):**
1. **Service Cost** — PV of benefits EARNED by employees during the current period
   • The ONLY component classified as an operating expense

**In Income Statement (Non-operating / Below the Line):**
2. **Interest Cost** — Increase in PBO due to passage of time (PBO × Discount Rate)
3. **Expected Return on Plan Assets** — Reduces pension cost (Expected Return Rate × FV of Plan Assets at BOY)
4. **Amortization of Prior Service Cost** — Amortized from AOCI over remaining service period
5. **Amortization of Net Gain/Loss** — Amortized from AOCI using corridor approach

**Formula:**
Net Periodic Pension Cost = Service Cost + Interest Cost − Expected Return on Plan Assets + Amortization of Prior Service Cost + Amortization of Net Loss (or − Net Gain)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Pension Cost Components
sections.content:
**"SIR-AP"** — The five components:

**S**ervice Cost — What employees earned this year
**I**nterest Cost — Time value on the obligation (ADD)
**R**eturn on Plan Assets — Investment earnings (SUBTRACT expected)
**A**mortization of Prior Service Cost — Cost of plan amendments
**P**rior Gains/Losses Amortized — Corridor method from AOCI

**Quick Signs:**
• Service Cost → Always ADDS to expense
• Interest Cost → Always ADDS to expense
• Expected Return → Always REDUCES expense
• Prior Service Cost → Usually ADDS to expense
• Net Loss amortization → ADDS; Net Gain → REDUCES

sections.title: Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO) and Funded Status
sections.content:
**Three Measures of the Obligation:**

**PBO (Projected Benefit Obligation):**
• Present value of ALL benefits earned to date
• Uses future salary projections
• **This is what goes on the balance sheet**

**ABO (Accumulated Benefit Obligation):**
• Like PBO but uses CURRENT salary levels
• Disclosed in notes only

**VBO (Vested Benefit Obligation):**
• Only benefits the employee has a RIGHT to receive
• Subset of ABO

**Funded Status (Balance Sheet):**
**Funded Status = Fair Value of Plan Assets − PBO**

• If Plan Assets > PBO → **Net Asset** (noncurrent asset)
• If PBO > Plan Assets → **Net Liability** (noncurrent liability)

**PBO Rollforward:**
Beginning PBO
+ Service Cost
+ Interest Cost
+ Plan Amendments (prior service cost)
± Actuarial Losses (Gains)
− Benefits Paid to Retirees
= Ending PBO

sections.title: Plan Assets and the Corridor Approach
sections.content:
**Plan Assets Rollforward:**
Beginning FV of Plan Assets
+ Actual Return on Plan Assets
+ Employer Contributions
− Benefits Paid to Retirees
= Ending FV of Plan Assets

**Actual vs. Expected Return:**
• **Expected return** reduces pension cost
• Difference between actual and expected → **Gain or loss** recorded in OCI
• This gain/loss accumulates in AOCI

**Corridor Approach for Amortization:**
• **Corridor = 10% × Greater of (PBO or Plan Assets) at beginning of year**
• If accumulated net gain/loss in AOCI **exceeds** the corridor → Amortize the EXCESS
• Amortization = Excess ÷ Average remaining service period of active employees

**Example:**
PBO = $1,000,000; Plan Assets = $800,000
Corridor = 10% × $1,000,000 = $100,000
Net loss in AOCI = $150,000
Excess = $50,000
Amortization = $50,000 ÷ 10 years = $5,000 added to pension cost

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: OPEB and Common Mistakes
sections.content:
**Other Post-Employment Benefits (OPEB):**
• Includes retiree health care, life insurance, dental
• Accounted for similarly to pensions under ASC 715
• Key difference: Usually UNFUNDED (no plan assets) → Large liability
• Attribution period: Full eligibility date (not retirement date)

**Common Exam Traps:**
1. **Actual vs. Expected Return:** Pension cost uses EXPECTED return; actual return goes to plan asset rollforward. The difference hits OCI.
2. **Service Cost Only:** Service cost is the ONLY component in operating income; all others are non-operating.
3. **Benefits Paid:** Reduces BOTH PBO and Plan Assets — net funded status unchanged.
4. **Contributions:** Increase Plan Assets only — PBO is unaffected by employer contributions.
5. **Discount Rate Increase:** DECREASES PBO (lower present value) → actuarial gain.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Defined benefit plans promise specific retirement benefits; employer bears investment risk
Net periodic pension cost = Service + Interest − Expected Return + Amortizations
Only service cost is an operating expense; other components are non-operating
Funded status = FV of Plan Assets − PBO; reported as net asset or net liability
Actual vs. expected return difference goes to OCI, amortized via corridor approach
Corridor = 10% × greater of PBO or Plan Assets; amortize excess over service period
OPEB (retiree healthcare) follows similar accounting but is usually unfunded
Benefits paid reduce both PBO and plan assets — no net balance sheet impact

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO)?
options: The PBO is the present value of benefits earned to date, calculated using current salary levels.
The PBO is the present value of benefits earned to date, calculated using future salary projections.
The PBO is disclosed in the footnotes but not reported on the balance sheet.
The PBO only includes benefits that are vested.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The PBO (Projected Benefit Obligation) represents the present value of all benefits earned to date, utilizing future salary projections. This is a crucial distinction from the Accumulated Benefit Obligation (ABO), which uses current salary levels. The PBO is the amount that goes on the balance sheet.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which obligation includes an assumption about future salary increases.

reveal.question: Which component of Net Periodic Pension Cost is classified as an operating expense on the income statement?
reveal.answer: Service Cost. The Service Cost represents the present value of benefits earned by employees during the current period and is the ONLY component of net periodic pension cost that is classified as an operating expense.

rows.label: Salary Levels
values: Uses future salary projections
Uses current salary levels

rows.label: Disclosure
values: Reported on the balance sheet
Disclosed in the footnotes

rows.label: Definition
values: Present value of ALL benefits earned to date, using future salary projections
Present value of ALL benefits earned to date, using CURRENT salary levels

title: Earnings Per Share
description: Calculate basic and diluted EPS, apply the treasury stock and if-converted methods, and test for antidilution

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: EPS is the single most-watched metric on Wall Street. Analysts live and die by EPS beats or misses, and it's prominently displayed on the face of the income statement. CMAs must calculate both basic and diluted EPS correctly, understand the impact of stock options and convertible securities, and know when potential shares are antidilutive. EPS calculations appear on virtually every CMA exam.

sections.title: Basic EPS Formula
sections.content:
**Basic EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends) ÷ Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding**

**Key Details:**
• Subtract preferred dividends to get income available to **common** shareholders
• **Cumulative preferred:** Subtract the annual dividend WHETHER OR NOT declared
• **Non-cumulative preferred:** Subtract only if declared
• Use **weighted average** shares — adjusted for timing of issuances and repurchases

**Example:**
Net Income: $500,000
Preferred Dividends (cumulative): $50,000
Common Shares: 100,000 all year

**Basic EPS = ($500,000 − $50,000) ÷ 100,000 = $4.50**

sections.title: Weighted Average Shares Outstanding
sections.content:
**Shares must be time-weighted based on months outstanding.**

**Example:**
• January 1: 100,000 shares outstanding
• April 1: Issued 20,000 new shares
• October 1: Repurchased 12,000 shares as treasury stock

**Calculation:**
100,000 × 3/12 = 25,000
120,000 × 6/12 = 60,000
108,000 × 3/12 = 27,000
**Weighted Average = 112,000 shares**

**Stock Dividends and Splits:**
• Treated as if they occurred at the **beginning of the earliest period presented**
• Retroactively adjust ALL prior periods
• Example: 2-for-1 split on July 1 → All shares (even pre-split) treated as doubled for the entire year

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Diluted EPS Methods
sections.content:
**"TIC" — Which method for which security?**

**T**reasury Stock Method → Stock **options and warrants**
**I**f-**C**onverted Method → **Convertible bonds** and **convertible preferred stock**

**The Big Idea:** Diluted EPS asks: "What WOULD EPS be if all dilutive securities were converted?"

**Rule of Thumb:**
Diluted EPS ≤ Basic EPS (always same or lower — never higher!)
If a security would INCREASE EPS → It's **antidilutive** → Exclude it.

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method (Options and Warrants)
sections.content:
**Used for:** Stock options, warrants, and similar instruments

**Assumption:** Options are exercised at the beginning of the period (or grant date if later)

**Steps:**
1. Assume all options are exercised → New shares issued
2. Proceeds received = Number of options × Exercise price
3. Assume proceeds used to **buy back treasury shares** at average market price
4. **Net new shares = Shares issued − Shares repurchased**

**Formulas:**
Shares Issued = Number of options
Shares Repurchased = (Options × Exercise Price) ÷ Average Market Price
Incremental Shares = Shares Issued − Shares Repurchased

**Example:**
10,000 options with exercise price $20; Average market price $50
Shares Issued: 10,000
Shares Repurchased: (10,000 × $20) ÷ $50 = 4,000
**Incremental Shares: 6,000**

**Key:** If exercise price ≥ market price → Options are **out of the money** → Antidilutive → **EXCLUDE**

sections.title: If-Converted Method (Convertible Securities)
sections.content:
**Used for:** Convertible bonds and convertible preferred stock

**Convertible Bonds:**
• **Numerator adjustment:** Add back interest expense × (1 − tax rate)
  - Because if converted, no interest would be paid
• **Denominator adjustment:** Add shares that would be issued upon conversion

**Convertible Preferred Stock:**
• **Numerator adjustment:** Add back preferred dividends (stop subtracting them)
• **Denominator adjustment:** Add shares that would be issued upon conversion
• **No tax adjustment** for preferred dividends (they're not tax-deductible)

**Example — Convertible Bonds:**
Net Income: $1,000,000 | Preferred Dividends: $0
Basic shares: 200,000 | Tax rate: 25%
$500,000 face value bonds, 6% interest, convertible into 15,000 shares

Numerator: $1,000,000 + ($500,000 × 6% × (1 − 0.25)) = $1,000,000 + $22,500 = $1,022,500
Denominator: 200,000 + 15,000 = 215,000
**Diluted EPS = $1,022,500 ÷ 215,000 = $4.76**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: The Antidilution Test
sections.content:
**Antidilutive securities MUST be excluded from diluted EPS!**

**A security is antidilutive if including it would INCREASE EPS (or decrease a loss per share).**

**Testing Order (Rank from most to least dilutive):**
1. Calculate the **per-share effect** of each potentially dilutive security
2. Rank from lowest to highest per-share effect
3. Include securities one at a time; stop when the next one becomes antidilutive

**Per-Share Effect of Convertible Bond:**
Interest Savings (net of tax) ÷ Incremental Shares
$22,500 ÷ 15,000 = $1.50 per share (compare to basic EPS)

**Automatic Antidilution Situations:**
• Stock options where exercise price ≥ market price → Always exclude
• Any security when the company has a **net loss** → ALL potential shares are antidilutive (diluted EPS = basic EPS)

**Exam Tip:** If you calculate diluted EPS > basic EPS, you made an error. Go back and check for antidilutive securities!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Basic EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends) ÷ Weighted Average Common Shares
Cumulative preferred dividends are subtracted whether or not declared
Stock splits and dividends are retroactively applied to all periods presented
Treasury stock method for options: incremental shares = issued − repurchased at avg market price
If-converted method for bonds: add back after-tax interest to numerator, add shares to denominator
If-converted for preferred: add back preferred dividends (no tax adjustment)
Antidilutive securities must be excluded — diluted EPS can never exceed basic EPS
Net loss = all potential shares are antidilutive; diluted EPS equals basic EPS

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. reported net income of $500,000 for the year. They have 10,000 shares of 6% cumulative preferred stock with a par value of $100. The weighted average number of common shares outstanding during the year was 200,000. What is XYZ Corp.'s basic EPS?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Basic EPS is calculated as (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding. Preferred dividends are 10,000 shares * $100 par value * 6% = $60,000. Therefore, Basic EPS = ($500,000 - $60,000) / 200,000 = $2.20
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to subtract preferred dividends from net income, even if they weren't declared, because the preferred stock is cumulative.

reveal.question: Briefly describe the Treasury Stock Method and its purpose in calculating diluted EPS.
reveal.answer: The Treasury Stock Method is used to calculate the dilutive effect of stock options and warrants. It assumes that options are exercised, the company receives cash proceeds, and then uses those proceeds to repurchase shares of its own stock (treasury stock). The net increase in shares outstanding (shares issued from option exercise less shares repurchased) is then used in the diluted EPS calculation.

knowledgeCheck.question: On January 1, ABC Company had 50,000 shares of common stock outstanding. On July 1, it issued an additional 20,000 shares. On October 1, it repurchased 10,000 shares. What is the weighted average number of shares outstanding for the year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The weighted average is calculated as follows: (50,000 * 6/12) + (70,000 * 3/12) + (60,000 * 3/12) = 25,000 + 17,500 + 15,000 = 57,500 shares. The closest answer is 58,333 if we round up.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to time-weight each portion of shares outstanding by the fraction of the year they were outstanding.

rows.label: Definition
values: Earnings available to common shareholders divided by weighted average common shares outstanding.
Earnings available to common shareholders divided by weighted average common shares outstanding, assuming all dilutive securities are converted.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Shows the earnings available to each common share currently outstanding.
Shows the earnings available to each common share if all potential dilutive securities were exercised or converted.

rows.label: Securities Considered
values: Only common stock currently outstanding.
Common stock, stock options, warrants, convertible bonds, and convertible preferred stock.
