title: Contingencies and Commitments
description: Apply ASC 450 to recognize, measure, and disclose loss and gain contingencies, and handle subsequent events

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Lawsuits, product warranties, environmental cleanups, government investigations — contingencies can make or break a company's financial position. CMAs must know precisely when to accrue a loss, when to only disclose, and when to stay silent. Getting this wrong leads to SEC enforcement actions and audit failures. The CMA exam frequently tests the three-tier classification system and the subtle rules around gain contingencies.

sections.title: ASC 450 Contingency Framework
rows: More than remote but less than likely

sections.title: Loss Contingency Recognition Rules
sections.content:
**Accrue a loss contingency when BOTH conditions are met:**

1. **Probable** — It is likely that a liability has been incurred as of the balance sheet date
2. **Reasonably Estimable** — The amount of the loss can be reasonably estimated

**What to Accrue:**
• If a single best estimate exists → Accrue that amount
• If a range exists with NO best estimate → Accrue the **MINIMUM** of the range
• Disclose the nature and the range (or that a range cannot be determined)

**Journal Entry:**
Dr. Loss (or Expense)                    XXX
  Cr. Estimated Liability (or Accrued Liability)    XXX

**Example — Pending Lawsuit:**
Company estimates a probable loss between $2M and $5M, with no best estimate.
→ Accrue $2M (minimum) and disclose the range up to $5M in the notes.

**If Probable but NOT Estimable:**
→ No accrual, but MUST disclose the contingency in the notes.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Contingency Decision Tree
sections.content:
**"PER" — Three Questions in Order:**

**P**robable? → If YES, go to next question; if NO, check if Reasonably Possible
**E**stimable? → If YES, **ACCRUE** the loss; if NO, **DISCLOSE** only
**R**ange with no best estimate? → Accrue the **MINIMUM**

**For Losses:**
Probable + Estimable = ACCRUE ✅
Probable + Not Estimable = DISCLOSE 📝
Reasonably Possible = DISCLOSE 📝
Remote = IGNORE (usually) 🚫

**For Gains:** NEVER accrue. At most, disclose.

**Think of it as CONSERVATISM in action:**
"Accrue losses early, recognize gains late."

sections.title: Gain Contingencies
sections.content:
**The Asymmetric Rule (Conservatism in Practice):**

**Gain contingencies are NEVER accrued.** Recognition occurs only when the gain is **realized** — meaning the contingency is resolved and the gain is certain.

**Treatment:**
• **Probable gain:** Disclose in notes (but do NOT accrue)
• **Reasonably possible gain:** May disclose (not required)
• **Remote gain:** No action

**Why?** This reflects the conservatism principle — overstatement of assets and income is considered worse than understatement. Premature gain recognition misleads investors.

**Examples of Gain Contingencies:**
• Pending lawsuit where the company is the PLAINTIFF
• Tax refund claims under dispute
• Insurance recovery awaiting settlement

**When the gain is REALIZED** (e.g., court awards final judgment):
Dr. Receivable (or Cash)    XXX
  Cr. Gain on Litigation         XXX

sections.title: Specific Contingency Types
sections.content:
**Litigation Reserves:**
• Assess each case: probable → accrue; reasonably possible → disclose
• Attorney letter responses are key audit evidence
• May aggregate immaterial cases

**Product Warranties:**
• **Assurance-type (included in price):** Accrue estimated cost at time of sale (not a contingency — ASC 460)
• **Extended warranties (service-type):** Separate performance obligation under ASC 606 — defer revenue

**Environmental Liabilities:**
• Superfund/CERCLA: strict, joint and several liability
• Accrue when probable and estimable; use the minimum of the range
• Do NOT discount unless timing is fixed and reliable

**Guarantees (ASC 460):**
• Indemnification agreements, product guarantees
• Initial recognition at fair value of the guarantee obligation
• Subsequently measured at higher of fair value or contingent loss amount

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Subsequent Events and Disclosure Pitfalls
sections.content:
**Subsequent Events (ASC 855):**
Events occurring AFTER the balance sheet date but BEFORE financial statements are issued.

**Type 1 — Recognized Subsequent Events:**
• Conditions EXISTED at the balance sheet date
• **Adjust** the financial statements
• Example: Customer declared bankruptcy in January — AR existed at 12/31, write it down

**Type 2 — Non-Recognized Subsequent Events:**
• Conditions DID NOT EXIST at the balance sheet date
• **Disclose** in notes only (do not adjust)
• Example: Factory fire in February — building was fine at 12/31

**Common Exam Traps:**
1. **Range with no best estimate:** Accrue the MINIMUM — not the midpoint, not the maximum
2. **Probable but not estimable:** disclosure required, no accrual
3. **Gain contingencies accrued:** NEVER — this is wrong regardless of probability
4. **Remote contingencies disclosed:** Generally NO, except for debt guarantees (always disclose guarantees)
5. **Unasserted claims:** Accrue only if assertion is probable AND loss from the assertion is probable and estimable

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Loss contingencies: accrue when BOTH probable and reasonably estimable (ASC 450)
Range with no best estimate → accrue the minimum of the range and disclose
Gain contingencies are NEVER accrued — only disclosed when probable
Three tiers: Probable (>75%), Reasonably Possible, Remote
Subsequent events: Type 1 (adjust financials) vs. Type 2 (disclose only)
Environmental liabilities use minimum of range; generally not discounted
Guarantees recognized at fair value initially under ASC 460
Remote contingencies generally not disclosed — except for debt guarantees

knowledgeCheck.question: Apex Corp. is involved in a lawsuit where it is reasonably possible that they will lose. Management estimates the loss could range from $500,000 to $1,000,000, and no single amount within that range is more likely than any other. According to ASC 450, what amount should Apex Corp. accrue as a loss contingency?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When a range of loss is estimated and no amount within the range is more likely than any other, the minimum amount in the range should be accrued. In this case, that is $500,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the rule for estimating losses within a range.

reveal.question: Describe the proper accounting treatment for a gain contingency that is deemed probable but not yet realized.
reveal.answer: A probable gain contingency should be disclosed in the notes to the financial statements, but it should NOT be accrued. Gains are only recognized when realized.

sections.title: Loss Contingency: Probable vs. Reasonably Possible

rows.label: Recognition
values: Accrued (if reasonably estimable)
Disclosed in notes to the financial statements

rows.label: Likelihood
values: Likely to occur
More than remote but less than probable

rows.label: Impact on Financial Statements
values: Affects both the income statement and balance sheet (if accrued)
Affects only the notes to the financial statements

title: Business Combinations and Consolidations
description: Master the accounting for mergers, acquisitions, and consolidated financial statements

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: M&A activity creates massive accounting complexity! CMAs must understand how to account for acquisitions, recognize goodwill, and prepare consolidated statements. These skills are critical for financial analysis and strategic planning.

sections.title: What Is a Business Combination?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A transaction where an acquirer obtains control of one or more businesses

**Forms of Business Combinations:**
• Merger - Target absorbed into acquirer
• Acquisition - Target becomes subsidiary
• Asset purchase - Specific assets acquired

**Control Defined:**
• Generally >50% voting ownership
• Power to govern financial and operating policies

**Why It Matters:**
Consolidation requires presenting combined entities as if they were one economic entity

sections.title: Acquisition Method (ASC 805)
sections.content:
**Step 1: Identify the Acquirer**
• Entity obtaining control
• Usually (not always) the larger company

**Step 2: Determine Acquisition Date**
• Date when acquirer obtains control
• Usually closing date

**Step 3: Recognize and Measure Assets/Liabilities**
• Fair value at acquisition date
• Includes identifiable intangibles

**Step 4: Recognize Goodwill (or Bargain Purchase)**

Goodwill = Purchase Price - Fair Value of Net Assets Acquired

**If negative:** Bargain purchase gain (rare) - recognize immediately in income

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Goodwill Calculation
sections.content:
**"PIGS GAIN" Formula:**

**P**urchase price paid
**I**dentifiable net assets at fair value
**G**oodwill = P - I
**S**ubsequent impairment testing required

**GAIN** if I > P (negative goodwill = bargain purchase gain)

Remember: Goodwill is NOT amortized - it's tested for impairment!

sections.title: Consolidation Procedures
sections.content:
**Basic Principle:** Present parent and subsidiaries as single entity

**Steps for Consolidation:**

**1. Combine Financial Statements**
• Add all assets, liabilities, revenues, expenses

**2. Eliminate Intercompany Transactions**
• Intercompany receivables/payables
• Intercompany sales/purchases
• Intercompany profit in inventory
• Dividends from subsidiary to parent

**3. Eliminate Investment Account**
• Parent's investment = Sub's equity
• Recognize goodwill or bargain purchase

**4. Calculate Non-controlling Interest (NCI)**
• If parent owns <100%, minority shareholders exist
• NCI = Fair value of subsidiary × (100% - Parent ownership %)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Intercompany Eliminations
sections.content:
**Most Tested Topic: Intercompany Inventory Profit**

Parent sells inventory to Sub for $120,000 (cost: $100,000)
Sub still holds 25% of inventory at year-end

**Unrealized Profit:** $20,000 × 25% = $5,000

**Elimination Entry:**
DR Cost of Goods Sold $5,000
CR Inventory $5,000

**Key Point:** Only eliminate profit in ending inventory
**Direction matters:** Downstream (parent→sub) vs Upstream (sub→parent)

sections.title: Non-Controlling Interest
sections.content:
**Definition:** Equity in subsidiary not attributable to parent

**Balance Sheet Presentation:**
• Separate component of equity
• Labeled "NCI" or "Minority Interest"

**Income Statement:**
• Net income split between:
  - Income attributable to parent
  - Income attributable to NCI

**Example:**
Parent owns 80% of Subsidiary
Subsidiary net income: $500,000

Parent's share: $400,000 (80%)
NCI share: $100,000 (20%)

sections.title: Goodwill Impairment
sections.content:
**Under ASC 350 (Simplified):**

**Step 1:** Compare fair value of reporting unit to carrying amount

**If FV < Carrying Amount:**
• Recognize impairment loss
• Loss = Carrying amount - Fair value
• But not exceeding goodwill balance

**When to Test:**
• At least annually
• More frequently if triggering events occur

**Triggering Events:**
• Significant decline in stock price
• Operating losses
• Industry downturn
• Loss of key personnel

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Business combinations use the acquisition method exclusively
Goodwill = Purchase price - Fair value of net identifiable assets
Identifiable intangibles must be separable or arise from contractual rights
Consolidation eliminates intercompany transactions and balances
Non-controlling interest represents minority shareholders' equity
Goodwill is not amortized but tested annually for impairment
Impairment loss = Carrying amount - Fair value (limited to goodwill balance)

knowledgeCheck.question: Alpha Corp acquires 100% of Beta Inc for $5,000,000. Beta's identifiable assets have a fair value of $4,200,000 and its liabilities have a fair value of $800,000. What amount of goodwill should Alpha recognize?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Net assets = Assets - Liabilities. Goodwill is the excess of purchase price over net asset fair value.

reveal.question: Why must intercompany transactions be eliminated in consolidation?
reveal.answer: Intercompany transactions are eliminated because consolidated statements present the group as a single economic entity. You cannot sell to yourself, owe yourself money, or earn profit from yourself. Without elimination, assets, revenues, and profits would be double-counted or artificially inflated.

title: Fair Value Measurement (ASC 820)
description: Master the framework for measuring and disclosing fair value in financial statements

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Fair value measurements affect everything from investment portfolios to pension obligations to impairment testing. CMAs must understand the fair value framework to ensure compliance, make proper estimates, and explain valuations to stakeholders.

sections.title: What Is Fair Value?
sections.content:
**ASC 820 Definition:**
The price that would be received to sell an asset or paid to transfer a liability in an orderly transaction between market participants at the measurement date.

**Key Concepts:**

**Exit Price (not Entry Price)**
• What you'd GET when selling
• Not what you'd PAY to buy

**Orderly Transaction**
• Not a forced or distressed sale
• Normal market conditions

**Market Participants**
• Buyers/sellers in principal market
• Independent, knowledgeable, willing

**Measurement Date**
• Balance sheet date typically

sections.title: Fair Value Hierarchy
rows: Quoted prices in active markets for identical items
Interest rates, yield curves, similar asset prices

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Fair Value Levels
sections.content:
**"1-2-3" = Easy to Hard:**

**Level 1** = "Look it up" (quoted price for same thing)
**Level 2** = "Figure it out" (adjust from observable data)
**Level 3** = "Make it up" (internal models, judgment)

**Think of reliability:**
1 = Most reliable (market prices)
2 = Moderately reliable (observable inputs)
3 = Least reliable (estimates)

**Must use highest level available!**

sections.title: Level 2 Inputs
sections.content:
**Observable but not Level 1:**

**Quoted Prices for Similar Assets**
• Comparable bonds
• Similar real estate

**Observable Market Data**
• Interest rates
• Credit spreads
• Yield curves
• Foreign exchange rates

**Derived/Corroborated Data**
• Dealer quotes
• Pricing services
• Broker quotes

**Key:** Must be observable - based on actual market data, not internal estimates

sections.title: Level 3 Measurements
sections.content:
**When No Market Data Exists:**

**Common Valuation Techniques:**
• Discounted cash flow (DCF)
• Option pricing models
• Multi-period excess earnings
• Relief from royalty

**Unobservable Inputs:**
• Company's own assumptions
• Risk-adjusted discount rates
• Growth projections
• Probability weightings

**Requirements:**
• Use best information available
• Consider market participant perspective
• Document methodology
• Extensive disclosures required

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Principal vs Most Advantageous Market
sections.content:
**Which Market to Use?**

**Principal Market:**
• Greatest volume and activity for the asset
• USE THIS FIRST if it exists
• Even if price is less favorable

**Most Advantageous Market:**
• Use ONLY if no principal market exists
• Maximizes amount received (assets)
• Minimizes amount paid (liabilities)

**Transaction Costs:**
• NOT part of fair value
• But used to determine most advantageous market

**Exam Tip:** If they mention both markets, focus on principal market.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Fair value = exit price in orderly transaction between market participants
Level 1: Quoted prices for identical items (most reliable)
Level 2: Observable inputs like similar assets or market rates
Level 3: Unobservable/internal inputs (least reliable, most judgment)
Use highest level inputs available
Principal market price prevails over most advantageous market
Transaction costs excluded from fair value measurement

knowledgeCheck.question: A company holds an investment in corporate bonds that are not actively traded but have similar bonds with observable market prices. The fair value measurement would most likely be classified as:
options: Level 1 - Quoted prices in active markets
Level 2 - Observable inputs other than quoted prices
Level 3 - Unobservable inputs
Not subject to fair value measurement
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Level 2 applies when you can use observable inputs from similar assets or other market data (like yield curves or credit spreads) to estimate fair value. Since similar bonds have observable prices, this provides Level 2 inputs for the illiquid bonds.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The bonds aren't actively traded (not Level 1), but similar assets provide observable data.

title: Segment Reporting and Decentralization
description: Report performance by business segments and evaluate decentralized operations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Large organizations need to understand performance at the segment level! Segment reporting provides visibility into which parts of the business create value and which destroy it. CMAs design segment reports and advise on responsibility center structures.

sections.title: ASC 280 Operating Segments
sections.content:
**GAAP Definition (for external reporting):**

An operating segment is a component that:
• Engages in revenue-generating activities
• Has discrete financial information
• Is regularly reviewed by chief operating decision maker (CODM)

**Quantitative Thresholds (10% tests):**
Segment is reportable if it meets ANY of:
• Revenue ≥ 10% of combined revenue
• Profit/loss ≥ 10% of combined profit (or loss)
• Assets ≥ 10% of combined assets

**75% Rule:**
Reported segments must cover at least 75% of external revenue

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Responsibility Centers
sections.content:
**"CRPI" - From least to most autonomy:**

**C**ost Center - Control costs (Production, IT)
**R**evenue Center - Control revenue (Sales team)
**P**rofit Center - Control both (Business unit)
**I**nvestment Center - Control everything including capital (Division)

**Key Principle:** Only evaluate managers on what they control!

sections.title: Segment Margin Analysis
sections.content:
**Internal reporting format:**

**Revenue**
- Variable costs
= Contribution Margin
- Direct fixed costs (traceable)
= **Segment Margin**
- Common fixed costs (allocated)
= Net Operating Income

**Key Insight:**
• Segment margin = best measure of segment profitability
• Includes only costs traceable TO that segment
• Common costs wouldn't disappear if segment closed
• Don't hold managers responsible for allocated costs!

sections.title: Traceable vs Common Costs
sections.content:
**Traceable (Direct) Fixed Costs:**
• Exist BECAUSE of segment
• Would disappear if segment closed
• Examples: Segment manager salary, segment advertising

**Common Fixed Costs:**
• Support multiple segments
• Would NOT disappear if one segment closed
• Examples: Corporate HQ, CEO salary

**Why it matters:**
Traceable costs are relevant for segment decisions
Common costs are allocated = artificial

**Segment could have:**
• Positive segment margin (keep open)
• But negative net income (due to allocations)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Fixed Cost Behavior
sections.content:
**A cost can be:**
• Fixed overall but DIRECT to segment
• Example: Segment manager's salary is fixed but traceable

**Don't confuse:**
• Variable/Fixed (cost behavior)
• Direct/Indirect (traceability)

**Example:**
Manager salary: Fixed AND Direct (traceable)
Factory rent: Fixed but may be Common (shared)
Direct materials: Variable AND Direct

**Segment margin includes ALL traceable costs - both variable AND fixed!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ASC 280 requires segment reporting if 10% thresholds met
Responsibility centers: Cost, Revenue, Profit, Investment
Evaluate managers only on what they control
Segment margin = Revenue - Variable costs - Traceable fixed costs
Traceable costs disappear if segment closes
Common costs are allocated and don't affect segment decisions
Positive segment margin = keep segment open (usually)

knowledgeCheck.question: A segment has: Revenue $500,000; Variable costs $300,000; Traceable fixed costs $150,000; Allocated common costs $80,000. Should this segment be kept open or closed?
options: Close - it loses $30,000
Keep open - segment margin is positive $50,000
Close - segment margin is negative
Need more information about variable costs
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Segment margin = $500,000 - $300,000 - $150,000 = $50,000 (positive). The $80,000 allocated costs would continue even if segment closed, so they're irrelevant. With positive segment margin, the segment covers its traceable costs and contributes to common costs.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate segment margin (ignore allocated common costs) to make the decision.

title: Revenue Recognition Under ASC 606
description: Apply the five-step model for recognizing revenue from contracts with customers

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Revenue is the top line - and the most scrutinized! ASC 606 provides a comprehensive framework for when and how to recognize revenue. CMAs must understand this to ensure compliant reporting and to structure contracts that achieve business objectives.

sections.title: The Five-Step Model
sections.content:
**ASC 606 Revenue Recognition Process:**

**Step 1: Identify the Contract**
• Agreement with enforceable rights/obligations
• Both parties approve and commit

**Step 2: Identify Performance Obligations**
• Distinct goods or services promised
• Each promise = potential separate obligation

**Step 3: Determine Transaction Price**
• Amount expected to receive
• Consider variable consideration

**Step 4: Allocate Price to Obligations**
• Based on standalone selling prices
• Proportional allocation

**Step 5: Recognize Revenue**
• When performance obligation is satisfied
• Over time or at a point in time

sections.title: When Is a Good or Service Distinct?
sections.content:
**Two Criteria:**

**1. Capable of Being Distinct**
• Customer can benefit from it alone
• OR together with other available resources

**2. Distinct Within the Contract**
• Not highly dependent on other items
• Not significantly modifying other items
• Not highly interrelated with other items

**Example - Separately Distinct:**
• Cell phone + service plan = 2 obligations
• Phone can be used with any carrier

**Example - NOT Separately Distinct:**
• Custom software + implementation = 1 obligation
• Implementation significantly customizes software

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Over Time Recognition
sections.content:
**Recognize OVER TIME if ANY of these is true:**

**1. Simultaneous Benefit**
Customer receives and consumes benefit as you perform
(e.g., recurring cleaning services)

**2. Customer Controls Asset**
You're building something customer controls
(e.g., construction on customer's land)

**3. No Alternative Use**
Asset has no alternative use to you AND
You have enforceable right to payment for work done
(e.g., custom manufacturing)

**Otherwise:** Recognize at a POINT IN TIME (when control transfers)

sections.title: Variable Consideration
sections.content:
**When price isn't fixed:**

**Types:**
• Discounts, rebates
• Price concessions
• Incentives, bonuses
• Penalties
• Returns

**Estimation Methods:**
• Expected value (probability-weighted)
• Most likely amount

**Constraint:**
Only include variable consideration to extent
it's "highly probable" of NOT being reversed

**Example:**
$100,000 contract with $10,000 bonus if done early
70% chance of bonus

Include bonus? Only if highly probable won't reverse

sections.title: Allocating Transaction Price
sections.content:
**Based on Standalone Selling Prices (SSP):**

**Method 1: Observable Prices**
• What you actually sell items for separately
• Best evidence

**Method 2: Adjusted Market Assessment**
• What competitors charge
• What market would pay

**Method 3: Expected Cost Plus Margin**
• Your cost + reasonable margin

**Example:**
Bundle: $90 (software $60 SSP + support $40 SSP)
Ratio: 60:40
Allocate $90: Software $54, Support $36

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Contract Modifications
sections.content:
**When contract terms change:**

**Treat as SEPARATE contract if:**
• Scope increases due to distinct goods/services
• Price increases by standalone value

**Otherwise:**
• Treat as modification of existing contract
• Update transaction price
• Adjust remaining revenue recognition

**Common Exam Questions:**
• Change order on construction project
• Adding services to existing agreement
• Price reduction for remaining deliverables

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Five steps: Contract → Obligations → Price → Allocate → Recognize
Identify DISTINCT performance obligations
Recognize over time if criteria met; otherwise at point in time
Variable consideration: include only if highly probable won't reverse
Allocate bundle price based on standalone selling prices
Contract modifications may be separate or part of original
Focus on when CONTROL transfers, not payment or delivery

knowledgeCheck.question: A software company sells a license ($800 SSP), one year of support ($300 SSP), and installation ($100 SSP) for a bundled price of $960. How much revenue is allocated to the support?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Total SSP = $800 + $300 + $100 = $1,200. Support ratio = $300 / $1,200 = 25%. Revenue allocated to support = $960 × 25% = $240.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate each item as a percentage of total standalone prices, then apply to bundle price.

title: Lease Accounting Under ASC 842
description: Apply the new lease accounting standards for both lessees and lessors

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: ASC 842 brought most leases onto the balance sheet! CMAs must understand how lease classification affects financial statements, debt covenants, and key ratios. This is a significant exam topic with complex implications.

sections.title: ASC 842 Overview
sections.content:
**Big Change:**
Under old rules (ASC 840), operating leases were off-balance sheet
Under ASC 842, almost ALL leases go on balance sheet

**Lessee Accounting:**
• Recognize Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset
• Recognize Lease Liability
• Both equal to PV of lease payments

**Two Lease Classifications for Lessees:**
• Finance Lease (like old capital lease)
• Operating Lease (new treatment)

**Key Difference:**
How expense is recognized on income statement

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Finance Lease Criteria
sections.content:
**"OWNS" - Any ONE of these makes it a Finance Lease:**

**O**wnership transfers at end

**W**ritten purchase option (bargain)
• Purchase option reasonably certain to exercise

**N**ear entire life (75% of useful life)
• Lease term ≥ 75% of asset's useful life

**S**ubstantially all value (90% of FMV)
• PV of payments ≥ 90% of asset's fair value

**PLUS:** Specialized asset with no alternative use

**If NONE apply:** Operating Lease

sections.title: Operating Lease Accounting Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• 5-year lease, $10,000 annual payment (year-end)
• Discount rate: 6%
• PV of payments: $42,124

**Inception Entry:**
Dr: ROU Asset          $42,124
Cr: Lease Liability    $42,124

**Year 1: Operating Lease Approach**
Lease Expense = $42,124 / 5 = $8,425 (straight-line)
Interest = $42,124 × 6% = $2,527
Principal = $10,000 - $2,527 = $7,473
ROU Amort = $8,425 - $2,527 = $5,898

**Entry:**
Dr: Lease Expense       $8,425
Dr: Lease Liability     $7,473
Cr: ROU Asset           $5,898
Cr: Cash               $10,000

sections.title: Balance Sheet Impact
sections.content:
**Before ASC 842:**
• Operating leases: Off balance sheet
• Only rent expense on income statement
• Better leverage ratios

**After ASC 842:**
• Operating leases: ON balance sheet
• Both asset and liability recorded
• May impact debt covenants!

**Ratio Impacts:**
• Debt-to-Equity: INCREASES
• Asset Turnover: DECREASES
• Current Ratio: May decrease
• EBITDA: May increase (lease expense reclassified)

**CMA Role:**
• Analyze covenant impacts
• Renegotiate if necessary
• Communicate to stakeholders

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Short-Term Lease Exception
sections.content:
**ASC 842 allows practical expedients:**

**Short-Term Lease (≤12 months):**
• Can elect to NOT capitalize
• Expense as incurred
• No ROU asset or liability

**Low-Value Asset (IFRS 16 only):**
• Value ≤ $5,000 (approximately)
• Not in US GAAP!

**What counts in lease term?**
• Non-cancellable period
• Renewal options reasonably certain
• Termination options NOT reasonably certain

**Include in PV calculation:**
• Fixed payments
• Variable payments based on index
• Purchase option if reasonably certain

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ASC 842: Most leases now on balance sheet (ROU Asset + Liability)
Finance vs Operating affects expense pattern, not balance sheet treatment
OWNS test: Ownership, Written option, Near entire life, Substantially all value
Operating lease: Straight-line expense; Finance lease: Front-loaded
Short-term leases (≤12 months) can be expensed directly
Impacts debt covenants, leverage ratios, asset turnover
Discount rate: Implicit rate or incremental borrowing rate

knowledgeCheck.question: A company enters a 4-year lease for equipment with a 10-year useful life. The PV of lease payments is $80,000 and the equipment's fair value is $100,000. No purchase option or ownership transfer. How should this be classified?
options: Finance lease - PV exceeds 90% threshold
Finance lease - term is 40% of useful life
Operating lease - no OWNS criteria met
Short-term lease - expense directly
knowledgeCheck.explanation: PV is 80% of FMV (below 90% threshold). Lease term is 4/10 = 40% of useful life (below 75% threshold). No ownership transfer or purchase option mentioned. None of the OWNS criteria are met, so this is an Operating Lease.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Check each OWNS criterion: Ownership transfer? Written option? Near entire life (75%)? Substantially all value (90%)?

title: Inventory Valuation Methods
description: Compare FIFO, LIFO, weighted average, and their financial statement impacts

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Inventory valuation directly affects both the balance sheet AND income statement! CMAs must understand how different methods impact cost of goods sold, profits, taxes, and working capital. This is a foundational topic across multiple CMA exam areas.

sections.title: Inventory Cost Flow Assumptions
sections.content:
**FIFO (First-In, First-Out):**
• Oldest costs go to COGS first
• Ending inventory at recent costs
• Matches physical flow for many businesses

**LIFO (Last-In, First-Out):**
• Most recent costs go to COGS first
• Ending inventory at oldest costs
• US GAAP only (not IFRS)

**Weighted Average:**
• Average cost per unit
• Applied to both COGS and ending inventory
• Periodic: One calculation per period
• Perpetual: Recalculate after each purchase

**Specific Identification:**
• Track actual cost of each item
• Used for unique, high-value items

sections.title: FIFO vs LIFO in Rising Prices
🧠 Memory Aid: FIFO vs LIFO Effects
sections.content:
**In RISING prices, remember "FIFO = Higher Everything":**

• Higher ending inventory
• Higher gross profit
• Higher net income
• Higher taxes (bad for cash!)

**LIFO = Lower Everything:**
• Lower ending inventory
• Lower gross profit
• Lower net income
• Lower taxes (good for cash!)

**In FALLING prices:** Reverse all effects!

**LIFO Conformity Rule:**
If you use LIFO for taxes, must use for GAAP reporting

sections.title: LIFO Reserve and Liquidation
sections.content:
**LIFO Reserve:**
• Difference between LIFO and FIFO inventory values
• Disclosed in notes
• Allows conversion between methods

**Converting LIFO to FIFO:**
FIFO Inventory = LIFO Inventory + LIFO Reserve
FIFO COGS = LIFO COGS - Increase in LIFO Reserve

**LIFO Liquidation:**
• Occurs when inventory quantities decrease
• Old, low-cost layers flow to COGS
• Results in artificially high profits
• Tax hit from liquidation
• Sometimes intentional, sometimes forced

sections.title: Weighted Average Method
sections.content:
**Periodic Weighted Average:**

Weighted Avg Cost = Total Cost of Goods Available / Total Units Available

**Example:**
| Purchase | Units | Cost | Total |
|----------|-------|------|-------|
| Beginning | 100 | $10 | $1,000 |
| June | 200 | $12 | $2,400 |
| October | 100 | $15 | $1,500 |
| Total | 400 | | $4,900 |

**Weighted Avg = $4,900 / 400 = $12.25/unit**

If 150 units sold:
COGS = 150 × $12.25 = $1,837.50
Ending Inv = 250 × $12.25 = $3,062.50

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Lower of Cost or NRV
sections.content:
**Inventory must be written down if impaired:**

**Under US GAAP:**
• Lower of Cost or Market (LCM)
• Market = Replacement cost
• Ceiling: NRV
• Floor: NRV - Normal profit margin

**Under IFRS:**
• Lower of Cost or NRV (simplified)
• NRV = Selling price - Costs to complete and sell

**Write-down:**
• Recognized as loss in current period
• Under IFRS: Can reverse if conditions improve
• Under US GAAP: No reversal allowed

**Exception:** LIFO companies use LCM, but reversal issues differ.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FIFO: Oldest costs to COGS; Ending inventory at recent costs
LIFO: Recent costs to COGS; Tax benefit in rising prices (US GAAP only)
Rising prices: FIFO = Higher income, Higher taxes; LIFO = opposite
Weighted average: Smooths out price fluctuations
LIFO Reserve = FIFO Inventory - LIFO Inventory
LIFO liquidation creates artificial profit from old layers
Lower of Cost or NRV/Market: Write down impaired inventory

knowledgeCheck.question: In a period of rising prices, which inventory method results in the highest cash flow from operations?
options: FIFO
LIFO
Weighted Average
Specific Identification
knowledgeCheck.explanation: LIFO produces lower taxable income in rising prices (recent higher costs go to COGS), which means lower income tax payments and therefore higher cash flow from operations. FIFO produces higher reported income but higher cash out for taxes.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Cash flow is affected by actual tax payments. Which method minimizes taxable income when prices rise?

title: Financial Statement Analysis Ratios
description: Master key ratios for liquidity, profitability, efficiency, and solvency analysis

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Ratio analysis is fundamental to CMA work! You'll use ratios to evaluate performance, benchmark against competitors, assess creditworthiness, and identify improvement opportunities. This is heavily tested - know the formulas AND interpretations.

sections.title: Ratio Categories
sections.content:
**Liquidity Ratios:**
• Can we pay short-term obligations?
• Focus on current assets vs current liabilities

**Activity/Efficiency Ratios:**
• How well do we use assets?
• Turnover and conversion cycles

**Profitability Ratios:**
• How much profit do we generate?
• Margin and return metrics

**Solvency/Leverage Ratios:**
• Can we meet long-term obligations?
• Debt vs equity structure

**Market Ratios:**
• What does the market think?
• P/E, book value, dividend yield

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DuPont Analysis
sections.content:
**ROE = Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Financial Leverage**

**Breakdown:**
• Net Income / Sales (Margin - Operating efficiency)
• Sales / Total Assets (Turnover - Asset efficiency)
• Total Assets / Equity (Leverage - Financial risk)

**Extended 5-Factor DuPont:**
• Tax Burden: NI / EBT
• Interest Burden: EBT / EBIT
• Operating Margin: EBIT / Sales
• Asset Turnover: Sales / Assets
• Financial Leverage: Assets / Equity

**Why it matters:** Identifies WHERE returns come from!

sections.title: Activity & Solvency Ratios
sections.content:
**Activity/Efficiency:**
• A/R Turnover = Credit Sales / Avg A/R
• Inventory Turnover = COGS / Avg Inventory
• Fixed Asset Turnover = Sales / Fixed Assets
• Total Asset Turnover = Sales / Total Assets

**Days Conversions:**
• DSO = 365 / A/R Turnover
• DIO = 365 / Inventory Turnover
• DPO = 365 / A/P Turnover
• Cash Conversion Cycle = DSO + DIO - DPO

**Solvency/Leverage:**
• Debt-to-Equity = Total Debt / Equity
• Debt Ratio = Total Debt / Total Assets
• Interest Coverage = EBIT / Interest Expense

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Ratio Interpretation
sections.content:
**Context matters more than absolute values!**

**Industry Differences:**
• Retail: Low margins, high turnover
• Luxury: High margins, low turnover
• Banks: Different ratios entirely

**Trend Analysis:**
• Is ratio improving or declining?
• What's causing the change?

**Manipulation Risks:**
• Window dressing at year-end
• Off-balance-sheet items
• Accounting policy choices

**Remember:**
• Compare to industry peers
• Compare to company history
• No single ratio tells full story

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four ratio categories: Liquidity, Activity, Profitability, Solvency
Current Ratio = CA/CL; Quick Ratio excludes inventory
DuPont: ROE = Margin × Turnover × Leverage
ROA measures asset efficiency; ROE measures equity returns
Cash Conversion Cycle = DSO + DIO - DPO
Interest Coverage = EBIT / Interest (minimum ~2.0x)
Always compare to industry and historical trends

knowledgeCheck.question: Company A has Net Margin of 8%, Asset Turnover of 1.5x, and Equity Multiplier of 2.0x. What is ROE?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: ROE = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier = 8% × 1.5 × 2.0 = 24%. DuPont analysis shows ROE comes from operating efficiency (margin), asset efficiency (turnover), and financial leverage (multiplier).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Simply multiply the three factors together.

title: Consolidation Accounting Basics
description: Understand when and how to consolidate subsidiary financial statements

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Most large companies have multiple legal entities! CMAs must understand consolidation to prepare group financial statements and analyze multi-entity performance. This affects everything from reporting to planning to performance measurement.

sections.title: When to Consolidate
sections.content:
**Consolidation Required:**
• Parent owns > 50% of subsidiary (control)
• Variable interest entities (VIEs)
• Effective control without majority ownership

**Equity Method (not consolidated):**
• 20-50% ownership (significant influence)
• Report investment + share of income

**Fair Value/Cost Method:**
• < 20% ownership
• Report investment at FV or cost

**Key Concept:**
Consolidation = Combine as if ONE entity
Equity Method = One line on balance sheet

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Intercompany Eliminations
sections.content:
**What must be eliminated:**

**Balance Sheet:**
• Investment in subsidiary vs Equity of subsidiary
• Intercompany receivables/payables
• Intercompany loans

**Income Statement:**
• Intercompany sales/COGS
• Intercompany interest
• Intercompany dividends
• Intercompany service fees

**Why eliminate?**
You can't sell to yourself!
• Consolidated sales = Sales to EXTERNAL parties
• A/R = Amounts owed by EXTERNAL parties
• Profit = Earned from OUTSIDE the group

sections.title: Goodwill Calculation
sections.content:
**Goodwill = Purchase Price - Fair Value of Net Assets**

**Example:**
• Parent pays: $1,000,000
• Subsidiary's book value: $600,000
• Fair value adjustments: +$150,000
• Fair value of net assets: $750,000
• Goodwill: $1,000,000 - $750,000 = $250,000

**Fair Value Adjustments:**
• Inventory stepped up
• Fixed assets revalued
• Intangibles identified
• Contingencies recognized

**Goodwill Treatment:**
• Not amortized
• Tested annually for impairment
• Impairment = Write down (no reversal)

sections.title: Non-Controlling Interest (NCI)
sections.content:
**What is NCI?**
• Outside ownership in consolidated subsidiary
• Parent owns 80% → NCI owns 20%

**Presentation:**
• Balance Sheet: NCI shown in equity section
• Income Statement: NCI share of net income shown

**Example:**
Subsidiary net income: $100,000
Parent ownership: 80%
NCI ownership: 20%

Income to parent: $80,000
Income to NCI: $20,000
Consolidated NI: $100,000 (but $20,000 goes to NCI)

**CMAs:** Track which profits belong to the group

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: 100% Elimination
sections.content:
**Eliminate 100% of intercompany transactions!**

Even if parent only owns 80%:
• Eliminate ALL intercompany sales
• Eliminate ALL intercompany profits
• Eliminate ALL intercompany balances

**Why 100%?**
Consolidation shows group AS ONE entity
• A company can't have receivables from itself
• A company can't sell to itself

**Common Error:**
Trying to eliminate only parent's share
→ WRONG! Eliminate everything
→ NCI gets their share of what remains

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Consolidate when > 50% ownership (control)
Equity method for 20-50% ownership (significant influence)
Eliminate ALL intercompany transactions (100%)
Goodwill = Price paid - FV of net assets acquired
Test goodwill for impairment annually
NCI = Outside ownership shown in equity
Allocate subsidiary income between parent and NCI

knowledgeCheck.question: Parent Co. acquires 100% of Sub Co. for $500,000. Sub's book value is $350,000, but fair value of identifiable net assets is $420,000. What is the goodwill?
options: $0 (negative goodwill)
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Goodwill = Purchase price - Fair value of net assets = $500,000 - $420,000 = $80,000. Note that book value ($350,000) is not used; we use fair value of identifiable net assets. The $70,000 difference between FV and book value represents fair value adjustments.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Use fair value of net assets, not book value.

title: Pension and Post-Retirement Benefits
description: Understand accounting for defined benefit and defined contribution plans

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Pension obligations can be massive - sometimes larger than market cap! CMAs must understand how pension accounting works for financial reporting, analysis, and M&A due diligence. Funded status and assumptions significantly impact financial statements.

sections.title: Plan Types
sections.content:
**Defined Contribution (DC):**
• Employer contributes specified amount
• Employee bears investment risk
• 401(k) is most common
• Accounting: Easy - expense = contribution

**Defined Benefit (DB):**
• Employer promises specified benefit
• Employer bears investment risk
• Traditional pension plans
• Accounting: Complex - actuarial calculations

**Trend:**
DB plans declining (risk to employer)
DC plans increasing (risk to employee)

sections.title: Defined Benefit Key Terms
rows: Projected Benefit Obligation - PV of all earned benefits
Fair value of assets set aside

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Pension Expense Components
sections.content:
**"SERAP" - Net Periodic Pension Cost:**

**S**ervice Cost
• Benefits earned this year
• Always expense

**E**xpected Return (credit)
• Return on plan assets
• Reduces expense

**R**ecognized gains/losses
• Amortization of deferred amounts

**A**mortization of prior service
• From plan amendments

**P**ressure from interest cost
• Interest on PBO
• Increases expense

**Net Pension Cost appears in Income Statement**
(Service cost in operating; others in non-operating)

sections.title: Balance Sheet Presentation
sections.content:
**Funded Status on Balance Sheet:**

If Plan Assets > PBO:
• Net Asset (prepaid pension)
• Overfunded status

If PBO > Plan Assets:
• Net Liability
• Underfunded status

**Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (AOCI):**
• Unrecognized gains/losses
• Unrecognized prior service cost
• Deferred in equity
• Amortized to income over time

**Example:**
PBO: $100 million
Plan Assets: $85 million
Funded Status: ($15 million) liability

sections.title: Key Assumptions
sections.content:
**Discount Rate:**
• Used to calculate PBO
• Based on high-quality bonds
• Higher rate = lower PBO

**Expected Return on Assets:**
• Affects pension expense
• Management judgment
• Higher rate = lower expense

**Salary Growth Rate:**
• Future compensation projections
• Affects PBO
• Higher rate = higher PBO

**Watch for:**
• Aggressive assumptions
• Changes year-to-year
• Comparison to peers

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Assumption Manipulation
sections.content:
**Assumptions significantly impact results!**

**To REDUCE liability/expense:**
• ↑ Discount rate
• ↑ Expected return on assets
• ↓ Salary growth rate

**To INCREASE liability/expense:**
• ↓ Discount rate
• ↓ Expected return on assets
• ↑ Salary growth rate

**Analysis Red Flags:**
• Assumptions differ significantly from peers
• Expected return much higher than actual
• Frequent assumption changes

**CMA Role:**
Understand assumption impacts
Challenge unrealistic assumptions

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: DC plans: Easy; expense = contribution
DB plans: Complex; actuarial calculations required
Funded Status = Plan Assets - PBO (on balance sheet)
Pension expense components: Service, Interest, Return, Amortization
Key assumptions: Discount rate, expected return, salary growth
Higher discount rate = Lower PBO and expense
Watch for aggressive assumptions vs. peers

knowledgeCheck.question: A company wants to reduce its reported pension liability. Which assumption change would accomplish this?
options: Decrease the discount rate
Increase the discount rate
Increase the salary growth rate
Decrease the expected return on assets
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Increasing the discount rate reduces the present value of the Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO), thereby reducing the liability. Think of it like bonds - higher discount rate means lower present value. Decreasing discount rate would increase the liability.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Higher discount rate means future obligations are worth less today.

title: Revenue Recognition Principles
description: Apply ASC 606 revenue recognition to common scenarios

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Revenue is the top line that drives everything! CMAs must understand when and how to recognize revenue correctly. ASC 606 brought major changes in 2018 and remains frequently tested. Incorrect revenue recognition is a leading cause of restatements.

sections.title: ASC 606 Five-Step Model
sections.content:
**Step 1: Identify the Contract**
• Agreement creating enforceable rights
• Approved by parties
• Commercial substance

**Step 2: Identify Performance Obligations**
• Distinct goods or services
• Each promise is a performance obligation

**Step 3: Determine Transaction Price**
• Amount expected to receive
• Include variable consideration

**Step 4: Allocate Price to Performance Obligations**
• Relative standalone selling prices

**Step 5: Recognize Revenue**
• When (point in time) or As (over time) performance obligation satisfied

sections.title: Performance Obligations
rows: No alternative use + right to payment

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Control Indicators
sections.content:
**Point in Time - Control Has Transferred When:**

**RIGHT TO PAYMENT:**
• Customer owes for asset

**LEGAL TITLE:**
• Ownership transferred

**PHYSICAL POSSESSION:**
• Customer has asset

**RISKS AND REWARDS:**
• Customer bears loss/obsolescence risk
• Customer gets upside benefits

**ACCEPTANCE:**
• Customer has accepted asset

No single indicator controls - use judgment!

sections.title: Variable Consideration
sections.content:
**Types:**
• Discounts
• Rebates
• Performance bonuses
• Rights of return
• Penalties

**Estimation Methods:**
• Expected value - probability-weighted
• Most likely amount - binary outcomes

**Constraint:**
Only include variable consideration if highly probable won't reverse
• Experience with similar contracts
• Uncertainty resolves quickly
• Limited factors outside control

**Update each period based on new information**

sections.title: Common Scenarios
sections.content:
**Bundled Goods/Services:**
• Separate performance obligations
• Allocate based on relative SSP
• Software + implementation + support

**Bill-and-Hold:**
• May recognize before delivery
• Substantive reason for arrangement
• Product separately identified
• Ready for transfer

**Consignment:**
• Don't recognize until customer sells
• Still control until sold

**Principal vs Agent:**
• Principal: Gross revenue
• Agent: Net (commission only)
• Who controls before transfer?

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Contract Modifications
sections.content:
**Contract changes affect revenue!**

**Treated as Separate Contract When:**
• Addition of distinct goods/services
• Price reflects standalone selling prices
• Account for separately, going forward

**Otherwise:**
• Cumulative catch-up adjustment OR
• Prospective treatment
• Depends on remaining goods/services

**Remember:**
Modifications require reassessment
May change remaining performance obligations

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ASC 606: Five-step model for revenue recognition
Performance obligation: Distinct promise to transfer good/service
Over time: Customer consumes benefits, or no alternative use + payment right
Variable consideration: Constrain to amount highly probable won't reverse
Allocation: Relative standalone selling prices
Principal recognizes gross; Agent recognizes net
Modifications: Separate contract or adjustment

knowledgeCheck.question: A company sells equipment for $100,000 and a 2-year service contract. Standalone prices: equipment $90,000, service $30,000 per year. How much revenue is recognized at equipment delivery?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Total standalone prices: $90,000 + $60,000 ($30K × 2) = $150,000. Equipment allocation: $100,000 × ($90,000 / $150,000) = $60,000. The remaining $40,000 is recognized over the 2-year service period.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Allocate based on relative standalone selling prices.

title: Shareholders' Equity Transactions
description: Account for stock issuances, treasury stock, and equity transactions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Equity transactions affect financial statements and EPS! CMAs must understand how stock issuances, buybacks, and stock compensation flow through the financials. These transactions directly impact key metrics and stakeholder analysis.

sections.title: Shareholders' Equity Components
sections.content:
**Contributed Capital:**
• Common stock (par value)
• Additional paid-in capital (APIC)
• Preferred stock

**Retained Earnings:**
• Accumulated profits
• Less dividends paid

**Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income:**
• Unrealized gains/losses
• Foreign currency translation
• Pension adjustments

**Treasury Stock:**
• Company's own repurchased shares
• Contra-equity account
• Reduces total equity

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Treasury Stock Methods
sections.content:
**Cost Method (Most Common):**

**Purchase:**
Dr Treasury Stock (cost)
    Cr Cash

**Reissue Above Cost:**
Dr Cash
    Cr Treasury Stock (cost)
    Cr APIC-Treasury Stock (excess)

**Reissue Below Cost:**
Dr Cash
Dr APIC-Treasury Stock (or R/E)
    Cr Treasury Stock (cost)

**Key Points:**
• Contra-equity (reduces equity)
• No gain/loss on treasury transactions
• Excess goes to APIC or R/E
• Outstanding shares decrease

sections.title: Stock-Based Compensation
sections.content:
**Stock Options:**
• Measure at grant-date fair value
• Recognize expense over vesting period
• No adjustment for subsequent changes

**Journal Entry (each period):**
Dr Compensation Expense
    Cr APIC - Stock Options

**At Exercise:**
Dr Cash
Dr APIC - Stock Options
    Cr Common Stock
    Cr APIC

**Restricted Stock:**
• Similar - expense over vesting
• Unvested = unearned compensation

**Forfeitures:**
• Reverse previously recognized expense

sections.title: Stock Dividends vs Stock Splits
sections.content:
**Stock Dividend:**
• Transfer from R/E to C/S
• Small (<20-25%): Market value
• Large (≥20-25%): Par value
• Shares increase, no cash moves

**Small Stock Dividend Entry:**
Dr Retained Earnings (market)
    Cr Common Stock (par)
    Cr APIC (excess)

**Stock Split:**
• No journal entry
• Just memo entry
• Par value decreases proportionately
• Shares increase

**Both:**
• No change in total equity
• More shares, lower price per share

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Par Value
sections.content:
**Par Value Quirks:**

**Par Value:**
• Stated value per share
• Usually nominal (e.g., $1, $0.01)
• Legal capital

**No-Par Stock:**
• No par value
• All proceeds to Common Stock (or stated value + APIC)

**Issuance at Discount:**
• Illegal in many states
• Contingent liability to creditors

**Key Calculation:**
APIC = Cash received - (Shares × Par value)

**Remember:**
Par value rarely equals market value!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Equity: Contributed capital + Retained earnings + AOCI - Treasury stock
Stock issued above par: Excess to APIC
Treasury stock (cost method): Contra-equity, no gain/loss recognized
Stock compensation: Expense over vesting at grant-date FV
Small stock dividend: Use market value; Large: par value
Stock split: Memo entry only, par value decreases
No gain/loss on company's own stock transactions

knowledgeCheck.question: A company reissues treasury stock (originally purchased at $40/share) for $50/share. Under the cost method, the $10 excess is credited to:
options: Gain on Sale of Treasury Stock
Retained Earnings
Additional Paid-in Capital
Revenue from Treasury Stock
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When treasury stock is reissued above cost, the excess is credited to Additional Paid-in Capital from Treasury Stock. Companies cannot recognize gains or losses on transactions in their own stock - it's a capital transaction, not income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Treasury stock transactions are capital transactions - no income statement impact.

title: Depreciation Methods and Asset Impairment
description: Calculate depreciation and test for impairment

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Depreciation affects profitability, taxes, and investment decisions! CMAs must understand how different methods impact financial statements and cash flows. Depreciation method choice can significantly affect performance metrics and tax planning. This topic is heavily tested on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Depreciation Basics
sections.content:
**Purpose:**
• Allocate cost over useful life
• Match expense to revenue
• NOT a valuation technique

**Key Components:**
• **Cost:** Purchase + Installation + Testing
• **Useful life:** Expected service period
• **Salvage value:** Expected residual value
• **Depreciable base:** Cost - Salvage

**Methods:**
• Straight-line
• Declining balance
• Units of production
• Sum-of-years-digits

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Double-Declining Balance
sections.content:
**DDB Calculation:**

**Rate = 2 / Useful Life**

**Annual Depreciation = Rate × Beginning Book Value**

**Example:** $100,000 asset, 5-year life
• Rate = 2/5 = 40%
• Year 1: 40% × $100,000 = $40,000
• Year 2: 40% × $60,000 = $24,000
• Year 3: 40% × $36,000 = $14,400

**Key Rules:**
• Don't subtract salvage initially
• BUT don't depreciate below salvage
• Often switch to SL in later years

**Why DDB:**
Matches expense to benefit for assets that decline rapidly

sections.title: Asset Impairment
sections.content:
**When to Test:**
• Significant decrease in market value
• Major physical damage
• Significant adverse change in use
• Operating/cash flow losses

**Two-Step Test (US GAAP):**

**Step 1 - Recoverability:**
IF Undiscounted future cash flows < Carrying amount
THEN impaired

**Step 2 - Measurement:**
Impairment loss = Carrying amount - Fair value

**Note:** Under IFRS, use recoverable amount (higher of fair value less costs to sell OR value in use)

sections.title: Tax vs Book Depreciation
sections.content:
**Book Purpose:**
• Match expense to revenue
• GAAP compliance
• Any systematic method

**Tax Purpose:**
• Minimize taxes
• IRS rules (MACRS)
• Accelerated methods preferred

**MACRS:**
• Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System
• Specific recovery periods by asset class
• Half-year convention typical
• No salvage value

**Result:**
Deferred tax liability when book income > tax income

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SYD Calculation
sections.content:
**Sum-of-Years-Digits:**

**SYD = n(n+1)/2**

For 5-year life: 5(6)/2 = 15

**Annual Rate:**
Year 1: 5/15
Year 2: 4/15
Year 3: 3/15
Year 4: 2/15
Year 5: 1/15

**Depreciation = (Cost - Salvage) × Fraction**

**Example:** $55,000 cost, $5,000 salvage, 5 years
• Depreciable base: $50,000
• Year 1: $50,000 × 5/15 = $16,667
• Year 2: $50,000 × 4/15 = $13,333

**Common Mistake:**
Using wrong numerator (remaining life, NOT year number)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Depreciable base = Cost - Salvage value
Straight-line: Equal expense each period
DDB: 2/Life × Book value, don't go below salvage
SYD: (Cost - Salvage) × Remaining/SYD
Impairment if undiscounted CFs < Carrying amount
Impairment loss = Carrying - Fair value
Tax depreciation (MACRS) differs from book

knowledgeCheck.question: Equipment costs $200,000, has 5-year life and $20,000 salvage. Using DDB, what is Year 1 depreciation?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: DDB rate = 2/5 = 40%. Year 1 depreciation = 40% × $200,000 = $80,000. Note: With DDB, we do NOT subtract salvage for the annual calculation (but we never depreciate below salvage).
knowledgeCheck.hint: DDB uses beginning book value, not depreciable base.

title: Lease Accounting Essentials
description: Account for operating and finance leases

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Lease accounting changed drastically with ASC 842! Nearly all leases now go on the balance sheet. CMAs must understand lease classification, measurement, and the financial statement impact. This affects key metrics like debt ratios and EBITDA.

sections.title: Lease Classification (Lessee)
sections.content:
**Under ASC 842:**
ALL leases > 12 months go on balance sheet

**Finance Lease (any ONE met):**
• Ownership transfers at end
• Purchase option likely exercised
• Lease term = major part of economic life
• PV of payments = substantially all of fair value
• Specialized asset with no alternate use

**Operating Lease:**
• All other leases
• Still goes on balance sheet
• Different expense recognition

**Short-term Exception:**
• ≤12 months: Can expense straight-line

sections.title: Lease Accounting - Initial
rows: Record at PV of lease payments + initial costs
Record at PV of lease payments
Rate implicit in lease OR incremental borrowing rate

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Finance vs Operating
sections.content:
**BOTH Types:**
• ROU Asset on balance sheet
• Lease Liability on balance sheet

**Finance Lease - Like Ownership:**
• Amortize ROU asset (declining)
• Interest expense on liability (declining)
• Total expense HIGHER early, LOWER late
• Front-loaded expense

**Operating Lease - Like Rental:**
• Single lease expense (straight-line)
• Total expense SAME each period
• Still amortize ROU and record interest
• But combine into one expense line

**Key Difference:**
Finance = Front-loaded expense pattern
Operating = Straight-line expense pattern

sections.title: Journal Entries Example
sections.content:
**At Inception (Both Types):**
Dr. Right-of-Use Asset
Cr. Lease Liability

**Finance Lease (Each Period):**
Dr. Interest Expense
Cr. Lease Liability

Dr. Amortization Expense
Cr. Accumulated Amortization - ROU

Dr. Lease Liability
Cr. Cash (payment)

**Operating Lease (Each Period):**
Dr. Lease Expense (straight-line)
Cr. Lease Liability (effective interest)
Cr. ROU Asset (plug)
Cr. Cash

(Simplified: One lease expense, but both asset and liability still adjust)

sections.title: Financial Statement Impact
sections.content:
**Balance Sheet:**
• Assets increase (ROU)
• Liabilities increase (lease liability)
• Debt ratios worsen

**Income Statement:**
• Finance: Amortization + Interest (lower net income early)
• Operating: Single lease expense (level)

**Cash Flow Statement:**
• Finance: Principal = Financing, Interest = Operating
• Operating: All payments = Operating

**EBITDA:**
• Finance lease: Unaffected (below EBITDA line)
• Operating lease: Add back lease expense for adjusted EBITDA

**Ratios:**
Be aware of impact on debt/equity, current ratio

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Old vs New Standards
sections.content:
**ASC 842 vs Old ASC 840:**

**Old (ASC 840):**
• Operating leases OFF balance sheet
• Only capital (finance) leases recorded
• Rent expense was straight-line

**New (ASC 842):**
• ALL leases ON balance sheet
• Except short-term (<12 months)
• Classification still matters for expense pattern

**CMA Exam Focus:**
• Current standard (ASC 842)
• Classification criteria (5 tests)
• Expense pattern differences
• Impact on ratios

**Remember:**
Classification affects INCOME STATEMENT
Both types affect BALANCE SHEET

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ASC 842: Nearly all leases on balance sheet
Finance lease: 5 criteria (any one = finance)
Finance lease: Front-loaded expense (amort + interest)
Operating lease: Straight-line expense
Both create ROU Asset and Lease Liability
Discount at implicit rate or incremental borrowing rate
Short-term exception: <12 months can expense as incurred

knowledgeCheck.question: Under ASC 842, an operating lease for a lessee results in:
options: No balance sheet impact
Asset only on balance sheet
Both ROU asset and lease liability on balance sheet
Liability only on balance sheet
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under ASC 842, BOTH finance and operating leases result in a right-of-use asset and lease liability on the balance sheet. The difference is in the expense pattern: operating leases have straight-line expense while finance leases have front-loaded expense.
knowledgeCheck.hint: What changed with ASC 842 vs the old standard?

title: Inventory Valuation Methods
description: Apply FIFO, LIFO, and weighted average methods

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Inventory method choice affects reported profits and taxes! CMAs must understand how different methods impact financial statements and make recommendations. This fundamental topic appears on both CMA Parts and is essential for cost analysis.

sections.title: Inventory Cost Flow Methods
sections.content:
**FIFO (First-In, First-Out):**
• Oldest costs to COGS first
• Newest costs remain in inventory
• Inventory = recent prices
• Allowed under GAAP and IFRS

**LIFO (Last-In, First-Out):**
• Newest costs to COGS first
• Oldest costs remain in inventory
• Better matching current costs to revenue
• US GAAP only (NOT IFRS)

**Weighted Average:**
• Average all costs
• Same cost for COGS and inventory
• Smooths price fluctuations
• Allowed under GAAP and IFRS

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: FIFO vs LIFO
sections.content:
**In RISING prices (inflation):**

**FIFO:**
• 'First prices' (low) go to COGS
• 'Last prices' (high) stay in inventory
• HIGHER profits, HIGHER taxes
• Inventory reflects current values

**LIFO:**
• 'Last prices' (high) go to COGS
• 'First prices' (low) stay in inventory
• LOWER profits, LOWER taxes
• Inventory understated

**Memory:**
'FIFO = Fat Income, Fat Inventory'
'LIFO = Lower Income, Lower taxes'

**In FALLING prices:**
Effects are REVERSED!

sections.title: LIFO Reserve and Conformity
sections.content:
**LIFO Reserve:**
= FIFO inventory - LIFO inventory
= Cumulative difference

**Use:**
• Convert LIFO to FIFO for comparison
• Estimate tax savings from LIFO
• Assess inventory understatement

**LIFO Conformity Rule:**
• If use LIFO for taxes, must use for financial statements
• IRS requirement
• Can disclose FIFO in notes

**LIFO Liquidation:**
• Selling into old LIFO layers
• Old, low costs flow to COGS
• Artificially high profits
• Increased taxes

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: LIFO and IFRS
sections.content:
**Critical Difference:**

**US GAAP:**
• FIFO, LIFO, weighted average all allowed
• Lower of cost or market (LCM)

**IFRS:**
• FIFO and weighted average ONLY
• LIFO NOT permitted
• Lower of cost or NRV
• Write-down reversal allowed

**Exam Questions:**
• May ask which method is prohibited under IFRS
• May ask to convert between methods
• May ask about LIFO liquidation effects

**Lower of Cost or Market (LCM):**
Market = Replacement cost
BUT: Not > NRV (ceiling)
Not < NRV minus normal profit (floor)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FIFO: Old costs to COGS, new costs in inventory
LIFO: New costs to COGS, old costs in inventory
Rising prices + FIFO = Higher income, higher taxes
Rising prices + LIFO = Lower income, lower taxes
LIFO not allowed under IFRS
LIFO reserve = FIFO inventory - LIFO inventory
LIFO liquidation causes artificial profit increase

knowledgeCheck.question: During a period of rising prices, which inventory method results in the LOWEST net income?
options: FIFO
LIFO
Weighted Average
All methods produce the same net income
knowledgeCheck.explanation: LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) assigns the newest, highest costs to COGS, resulting in the highest COGS and lowest net income during rising prices. This also results in the lowest tax expense and best cash flow.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which method sends the highest costs to expenses?

title: Financial Statement Analysis Ratios
description: Calculate and interpret key financial ratios

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Ratios transform raw financial data into meaningful insights! CMAs use ratio analysis to evaluate performance, identify trends, and benchmark against competitors. Mastering financial ratios is essential for both CMA exam success and professional practice.

sections.title: Ratio Categories
sections.content:
**Liquidity Ratios:**
• Can the company pay short-term debts?
• Current ratio, quick ratio, cash ratio

**Activity (Efficiency) Ratios:**
• How well are assets utilized?
• Turnover ratios, days ratios

**Leverage (Solvency) Ratios:**
• How much debt vs equity?
• Debt-to-equity, times interest earned

**Profitability Ratios:**
• How profitable is the company?
• Margin ratios, return ratios

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Liquidity Ratios
sections.content:
**Current Ratio:**
Current Assets / Current Liabilities
• Includes inventory
• General liquidity measure
• Target often > 2.0

**Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio:**
(Current Assets - Inventory) / Current Liabilities
• Excludes inventory (hard to liquidate)
• Stricter test
• Target often > 1.0

**Cash Ratio:**
(Cash + Marketable Securities) / Current Liabilities
• Most conservative
• Only most liquid assets

**Memory:**
Liquidity increases as we get more restrictive
Current > Quick > Cash ratio (in terms of coverage)

sections.title: Activity and Turnover Ratios
sections.content:
**A/R Turnover:**
Credit Sales / Avg A/R
• How many times A/R collected per year
• Higher = faster collection

**Days Sales Outstanding (DSO):**
365 / A/R Turnover
• Average collection period
• Lower = faster collection

**Inventory Turnover:**
COGS / Avg Inventory
• How many times inventory sold
• Higher usually better

**Days in Inventory:**
365 / Inventory Turnover
• How long inventory is held

**A/P Turnover:**
Purchases / Avg A/P
• How quickly paying suppliers

sections.title: DuPont Analysis
sections.content:
**DuPont Formula:**

ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier

ROE = (NI/Sales) × (Sales/Assets) × (Assets/Equity)

**Components:**
• Profit margin = Profitability
• Asset turnover = Efficiency
• Equity multiplier = Leverage

**Insight:**
Shows HOW ROE is achieved:
• High margins?
• Efficient asset use?
• High leverage?

**Use:**
• Compare companies
• Identify improvement areas

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Ratio Calculations
sections.content:
**Common Exam Mistakes:**

**Numerator/Denominator:**
• Turnover = Flow / Stock
• Flow from income statement (sales, COGS)
• Stock from balance sheet (averages!)

**Use Averages:**
• For balance sheet items used with IS
• (Beginning + Ending) / 2
• Matches timing of IS and BS

**COGS vs Sales:**
• Inventory turnover uses COGS (matches cost)
• A/R turnover uses Sales
• A/P uses Purchases (not COGS)

**Credit vs Total Sales:**
• A/R turnover ideally uses credit sales
• If not given, use total sales

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Ratios: Liquidity, Activity, Leverage, Profitability
Current ratio includes inventory; Quick ratio excludes it
Turnover = Flow item / Average stock item
Days = 365 / Turnover
DuPont: ROE = Margin × Turnover × Leverage
Always use average for B/S items with I/S items
Compare to industry benchmarks for context

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has $600K current assets, $200K inventory, and $300K current liabilities. What is the quick ratio?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Quick ratio = (Current Assets - Inventory) / Current Liabilities = ($600K - $200K) / $300K = $400K / $300K = 1.33. This excludes inventory to give a stricter liquidity measure.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Quick ratio excludes inventory from current assets.

title: Revenue Recognition under ASC 606
description: Apply the five-step revenue recognition model

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Revenue is the top line - and often the most scrutinized! ASC 606 revolutionized revenue recognition with a principles-based approach. CMAs must understand these rules for accurate financial reporting and performance measurement.

sections.title: ASC 606 Overview
sections.content:
**Background:**
• Effective 2018 (public companies)
• Replaces industry-specific guidance
• Converged with IFRS 15
• Single principles-based model

**Core Principle:**
Recognize revenue to depict transfer of goods/services to customers in an amount reflecting expected consideration

**Five-Step Model:**
1. Identify the contract
2. Identify performance obligations
3. Determine transaction price
4. Allocate price to obligations
5. Recognize revenue when satisfied

sections.title: Five-Step Model
rows: Point in time or over time

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Five Steps
sections.content:
**Memory:** 'Contracts Obligate Prices Allocated to Revenue'

**Step 1 - Contract:**
• Both parties approved
• Rights identified
• Payment terms exist
• Commercial substance
• Collection probable

**Step 2 - Performance Obligations:**
• Promise to transfer distinct good/service
• Distinct = Standalone value + separately identifiable
• Each bundle treated separately

**Step 3 - Transaction Price:**
• Amount expected to receive
• Excludes amounts collected for others (sales tax)
• Include variable consideration (estimates)

sections.title: Variable Consideration
sections.content:
**Types of Variable Consideration:**
• Discounts
• Rebates
• Returns
• Performance bonuses
• Penalties

**Estimation Methods:**
• **Expected value:** Probability-weighted amounts
  - Best when many possible outcomes
• **Most likely amount:** Single most likely outcome
  - Best when binary outcomes

**Constraint:**
Only include variable consideration to extent it's highly probable NO significant revenue reversal will occur

**Example:**
$100K contract + up to $20K bonus
If 60% probability of full bonus:
Expected value: $100K + ($20K × 60%) = $112K
But apply constraint - may recognize less

sections.title: Point in Time vs Over Time
sections.content:
**Over Time Recognition (if any criteria met):**
• Customer receives/consumes benefits simultaneously
• Asset has no alternative use + right to payment
• Company creates/enhances customer-controlled asset

**Point in Time (default):**
Recognize when control transfers

**Indicators of Control Transfer:**
• Right to payment
• Legal title passed
• Physical possession
• Risks and rewards transferred
• Customer accepted

**Examples:**
• Over time: Construction contract, SaaS subscription
• Point in time: Product sale delivered to customer

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Common Issues
sections.content:
**Watch for These:**

**Bill-and-Hold Arrangements:**
• Revenue only if customer requests
• Goods identified as belonging to customer
• Ready for transfer

**Consignment Sales:**
• No revenue until sold by consignee
• Seller retains risks

**Principal vs Agent:**
• Principal: Gross revenue
• Agent: Net revenue (commission only)
• Who controls goods before transfer?

**Multiple Performance Obligations:**
• Allocate based on standalone prices
• Recognize each separately

**Warranties:**
• Assurance-type: Part of product (accrue)
• Service-type: Separate obligation

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ASC 606: Principles-based, five-step model for all industries
Step 1: Identify valid contract with customer
Step 2: Identify distinct performance obligations
Step 3: Determine transaction price (including variable)
Step 4: Allocate price to obligations (standalone prices)
Step 5: Recognize when obligation satisfied (over time or point)
Variable consideration subject to constraint

knowledgeCheck.question: A software company sells a license ($40K standalone price) bundled with one year of support ($20K standalone price) for $54K total. How much revenue is allocated to the license?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Allocate based on relative standalone prices. License = $40K / ($40K + $20K) = 67%. Revenue for license = $54K × 67% = $36,000. Support gets remaining $18,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Allocate the bundle price based on relative standalone selling prices.
